events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African descent, a retired schoolteacher, nonsmoker, with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (10 years, on metformin) and hypertension. BMI 24.1. No family history of pancreatic cancer. She lives alone but has strong family support.

At age 66 years, the patient was diagnosed with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), stage III (AJCC 8th edition, T4 N1 M0). Primary tumor site: head and uncinate process of the pancreas (ICD-O-3 site code C25.0; histology code 8500/3, infiltrating duct carcinoma NOS). Summary stage: Regional. Tumor measured 3.8 cm in greatest dimension on CT. The tumor abutted the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over 180 degrees of circumference and was in direct contact with the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with partial occlusion. One regional lymph node was involved (peripancreatic). CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL (markedly elevated). CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. Confirmation: Positive histology by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy. No distant metastases identified. Performance status ECOG 1. New-onset diabetes worsening noted in preceding 3 months, prompting workup.

At age 66 years, the patient underwent EUS-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology showed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (infiltrating duct carcinoma, grade 2). Tumor cells showed prominent desmoplastic stroma. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative, SMAD4 (DPC4) loss of expression by IHC, consistent with homozygous deletion or inactivating mutation. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 35%. No perineural invasion assessable on this biopsy specimen. Findings confirmed primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

At age 66 years, the patient had a multiphasic CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol). Findings: Hypoenhancing mass in the head/uncinate process of the pancreas measuring 3.8 x 3.2 cm. Tumor contact with the SMA greater than 180 degrees. Direct abutment and partial encasement of the SMV with mild luminal narrowing but no complete occlusion. Moderate dilation of the main pancreatic duct (6 mm) and mild intrahepatic biliary dilation with CBD diameter 12 mm. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. One peripancreatic lymph node 1.2 cm suspicious for involvement. No distant organ metastases. Impression: Borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV involvement render immediate resection unfeasible.

At age 66 years, the patient had a PET-CT (FDG) scan for staging. Findings: Intense FDG-avid mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate (SUVmax 8.9). One FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2). No hepatic FDG-avid lesions. No pulmonary nodules with uptake. No peritoneal FDG avidity. No bone lesions. Impression: FDG-avid primary pancreatic mass with regional nodal involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Consistent with locoregional pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

At age 66 years, next generation sequencing was performed on the EUS-guided biopsy specimen from the pancreatic head mass (tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling, 324-gene panel). Actionable and clinically relevant alterations: KRAS p.G12V mutation (VAF 42%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver), TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 38%, pathogenic), SMAD4 homozygous deletion (copy number 0, consistent with IHC finding), CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Additional alterations: ARID1A p.R1276* nonsense mutation (VAF 12%), RNF43 p.G659fs frameshift mutation (VAF 8%), ATM p.V2716fs frameshift mutation (VAF 6%, germline vs somatic uncertain - germline testing recommended), BRCA2 splice site variant c.8331+1G>A (VAF 5%, low VAF - likely subclonal or technical artifact; germline testing recommended). TMB: 3 mutations/Mb (low). MSI status: MSI-stable (MSS). PD-L1 CPS: 2 (low). No NTRK fusions. No FGFR alterations. No HER2 amplification. No ALK or RET fusions. Copy number alterations: SMAD4 deletion, CDKN2A deletion, MYC gain (low level). Tumor mutational burden: Low. Note: ATM frameshift variant of uncertain germline significance prompts germline testing.

At age 66 years, the patient was evaluated by the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board. Case reviewed by surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology. Consensus: Borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma due to SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV involvement. Recommendation for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) given good performance status (ECOG 1), adequate hepatic function, and absence of distant metastases. Plan for biliary decompression if bilirubin rises. Genetic counseling referral placed given ATM and low-VAF BRCA2 variants. Diabetes management optimization with endocrinology. Baseline CA 19-9: 1,247 U/mL. Restaging planned after 4 cycles. Cancer present. No prior therapy.

At age 66 years, the patient initiated neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX): oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2 given age and diabetes), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, omitting bolus 5-FU. Cycle 1 of planned 8 cycles. Prophylactic pegfilgrastim administered. Antiemetics: ondansetron, dexamethasone, olanzapine. Treatment intent: neoadjuvant, curative intent.

At age 66 years, after cycle 2 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias, oxaliplatin-related), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 diarrhea. Loperamide initiated for diarrhea. Cold sensitivity noted. Dose modification: oxaliplatin reduced by 20% (to 68 mg/m2) for subsequent cycles. Irinotecan maintained. No neutropenic fever. CA 19-9 after cycle 2: 620 U/mL (declining from baseline 1,247).

At age 66 years (approximately 3 months after diagnosis), the patient had a restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measuring 3.1 x 2.5 cm (previously 3.8 x 3.2 cm), representing partial response per RECIST 1.1 (approximately 22% decrease in sum of longest diameters). Improved desmoplastic halo around the tumor. SMA contact decreased to approximately 90-120 degrees of circumference. SMV contact with residual soft tissue but improved luminal caliber. Peripancreatic lymph node decreased to 0.7 cm, no longer clearly enlarged. No new lesions. Main pancreatic duct dilation improved (4 mm). Impression: Partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Tumor appears more favorably positioned relative to SMA. Continue neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 66 years, restaging clinical assessment after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. CA 19-9 declined from 1,247 to 310 U/mL (75% decline). Radiographic partial response confirmed. Patient ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 2 stable. No evidence of disease progression. Plan: Continue mFOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles (cycles 5-8) with oxaliplatin dose reduction in place. Reassess for surgical candidacy after completion of 8 cycles. Genetic counseling completed: germline testing ordered; ATM p.V2716fs confirmed germline pathogenic variant (heterozygous) - patient counseled on implications. BRCA2 variant not detected on germline testing (somatic only, subclonal). Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66 years, during cycles 5-8 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 3 fatigue requiring brief dose delay of 1 week after cycle 6. Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy persisted (stable). Grade 1 alopecia. Grade 1 mucositis managed with magic mouthwash. No grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity. No dose reduction beyond the prior oxaliplatin reduction was required. Patient completed all 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX with cumulative dose modifications.

At age 66 years (approximately 5 months after diagnosis), the patient had restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest after completing 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. Findings: Pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 x 2.1 cm (previously 3.1 x 2.5 cm at mid-treatment scan). Continued partial response. SMA contact now approximately 90 degrees or less of circumference. SMV shows residual soft tissue contact but vessel appears patent with no significant luminal compromise. No enlarged lymph nodes (largest 0.5 cm). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. No pulmonary nodules. Impression: Continued partial response after 8 cycles of neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. Tumor response and vascular anatomy now potentially favorable for resection after consolidative chemoradiation. Tumor remains in contact with SMA; multidisciplinary reassessment for resectability recommended.

At age 66 years, multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment after 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. CA 19-9 now 85 U/mL (93% decline from baseline of 1,247 U/mL). Radiographic partial response with tumor size reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm. SMA contact now approximately 90 degrees. Surgical team assessment: Patient potentially resectable but SMA contact remains borderline. Recommendation: Proceed with consolidative chemoradiation (long-course radiation with concurrent capecitabine) prior to surgical reassessment. ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 1-2 (improved). Plan for stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) vs. conventionally fractionated chemoRT discussed; consensus favored conventional long-course chemoRT with capecitabine for maximal local control. Cancer present, responding to neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 66 years (approximately 6 months after diagnosis), the patient underwent conventionally fractionated external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) to the pancreatic primary tumor and regional lymphatics. Technique: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) with daily image guidance (IGRT). Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5.5 weeks). Target volumes: primary gross tumor volume (GTV) encompassing pancreatic head mass and involved vascular margin, clinical target volume (CTV) including peripancreatic lymphatics and celiac axis nodal region. Concurrent radiosensitizer: capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on days of radiation. Treatment intent: consolidative neoadjuvant, curative intent. Fiducial markers placed for image guidance prior to simulation. Dosimetric constraints respected for duodenum, small bowel, stomach, liver, kidneys, and spinal cord.

At age 66 years, during chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 2 nausea and grade 2 fatigue, managed with ondansetron and dietary modifications. Grade 2 radiation-induced gastritis (epigastric pain, managed with proton pump inhibitor escalation). Grade 1 skin reaction at radiation field. Hand-foot syndrome grade 1 from capecitabine (bilateral plantar erythema, mild). No treatment interruptions required. Weight loss of 3 kg over the chemoradiation course. Nutritional counseling provided; oral supplementation initiated.

At age 66 years (approximately 2 months after completing chemoradiation, 8 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest prior to surgical reassessment. Findings: Pancreatic head mass measuring 2.3 x 1.9 cm (continued reduction from 2.6 x 2.1 cm). Tumor margin adjacent to SMA with contact now estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. SMV appears patent with only minimal soft tissue thickening at prior contact site. No new hepatic lesions. No enlarged lymph nodes. No peritoneal disease. No pulmonary metastases. CA 19-9: 72 U/mL. Impression: Further radiographic response after chemoradiation. Vascular anatomy now appears potentially favorable for R0 resection. Surgical evaluation for pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) recommended.

At age 66 years, pre-surgical assessment for pancreaticoduodenectomy. Multidisciplinary tumor board confirmed resectability based on radiographic response and CA 19-9 normalization trend. Surgical oncology: Technically resectable with intent for R0 (negative margin) resection. Anticipated need for SMV reconstruction not required based on imaging. Pre-operative optimization: HbA1c 7.8% (improved diabetes control). Nutritional status: mild pre-operative malnutrition, supplemental oral nutrition. Cardiopulmonary clearance obtained. Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation completed. Planned operation: classic pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with pylorus-preserving technique. ECOG 1. Cancer present, intent curative resection.

At age 66 years (approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD, Whipple procedure) with curative intent. Procedure performed by hepatobiliary surgical oncologist. Intraoperative findings: Primary tumor in pancreatic head/uncinate process, grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA. SMV was dissected free without need for venous reconstruction. No peritoneal implants identified. No liver metastases on intraoperative inspection. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin and common bile duct margin were negative for malignancy. Procedure: en bloc resection of pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, proximal 15 cm jejunum, gallbladder, distal common bile duct, and distal stomach remnant. Regional lymphadenectomy performed. Reconstruction: pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa anastomosis), hepaticojejunostomy, duodenojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss 650 mL; 1 unit packed red blood cells transfused. Operative time 5 hours 40 minutes. Jackson-Pratt drain placed near pancreaticojejunostomy.

At age 66 years, the surgical pathology from the Whipple resection specimen was reported. Final diagnosis: Moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head and uncinate process. Tumor size: 2.1 x 1.8 cm (significantly reduced from pre-treatment 3.8 cm). Extensive treatment effect: College of American Pathologists (CAP) tumor regression score 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident tumor regression). Margins: Pancreatic neck margin NEGATIVE (0.4 cm clearance). Common bile duct margin NEGATIVE. Posterior (retroperitoneal/SMA) margin NEGATIVE (0.1 cm - close but negative). Anterior surface NEGATIVE. Uncinate margin NEGATIVE (0.3 cm). All surgical margins negative (R0 resection). Lymph nodes: 0/22 lymph nodes positive for malignancy (ypN0). Lymphovascular invasion: present focally. Perineural invasion: present. Pathologic stage (post-neoadjuvant): ypT2 ypN0 M0, Stage IIA (AJCC 8th edition). Ampulla and duodenum: no tumor involvement. Proximal and distal bile duct margins: negative. Additional findings: Background chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) grade 1-2.

At age 66 years, on post-operative day 5 following the Whipple procedure, the patient was found to have biochemical leak from the pancreaticojejunostomy (drain amylase >3x upper limit of normal). This progressed to a clinically relevant grade B pancreatic fistula (International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula - ISGPF definition) by post-operative day 7: drain amylase 4,800 U/L, drain output 180 mL/day, low-grade fever 38.1°C, no systemic sepsis. Management: Drain left in place, bowel rest with transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN), octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneous TID initiated. Hospital stay extended to 18 days (standard 7-10 days). No radiologic or surgical intervention required. Fistula gradually resolved by post-operative week 5 with drain output decreasing and amylase normalizing. Patient discharged home on post-operative day 18 with drain still in situ; drain removed at 5-week post-operative clinic visit when output minimal.

At age 66 years, 6-week post-operative visit following Whipple procedure. Patient recovering well from grade B pancreatic fistula, which has resolved. Drain removed. Weight 3.5 kg below pre-operative weight. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency confirmed (steatorrhea, elevated fecal elastase <200 mcg/g); pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) initiated with meals (pancrelipase 40,000 lipase units per meal). New-onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus post-Whipple; endocrinology co-management. CA 19-9: 48 U/mL (post-operative nadir). ECOG performance status 1. Pathology reviewed: R0 resection, ypT2N0M0, CAP regression score 2. Adjuvant therapy discussion: Plan to initiate adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen) once patient recovered. Target start approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively. Cancer status: No cancer identified (R0 resection, pathologically node-negative).

At age 66 years (approximately 10 weeks post-Whipple, 11 months after initial diagnosis), the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen). Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle; capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21 of each cycle. Planned for 6 cycles (6 months). Treatment intent: adjuvant, curative intent. Baseline post-surgical CA 19-9: 48 U/mL. ECOG 1.

At age 66 years, during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (cycles 1-3), the patient developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (HFS; palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) from capecitabine, requiring capecitabine dose reduction to 75% (from 1660 mg/m2/day to 1250 mg/m2/day). Grade 2 fatigue persisted. Grade 1 myelosuppression (neutrophil nadir 1.2 x10^9/L, no febrile neutropenia). Pancreatic fistula-related sequela: Fluid collection near the pancreatic anastomosis identified on routine imaging (2.8 cm simple-appearing fluid collection), monitored conservatively without intervention. No signs of infection. Hand-foot syndrome improved to grade 1 after dose reduction.

At age 66 years (approximately 3 months post-operatively, during adjuvant therapy cycle 3), the patient had surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast. Findings: Post-surgical changes from Whipple procedure. Pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis intact. Small fluid collection adjacent to anastomosis measuring 2.8 cm (decreased from prior 3.2 cm on prior scan); simple in appearance, no internal debris or gas. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal nodularity. No enlarged lymph nodes. Surgical bed without evidence of local recurrence. No ascites. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules. Impression: Expected post-operative changes. Decreasing anastomotic fluid collection, likely post-surgical seroma/resolving fistula-related collection. No evidence of disease recurrence. CA 19-9: 32 U/mL.

At age 66 years, clinical assessment during adjuvant chemotherapy (after cycle 4 of 6 gemcitabine/capecitabine). Patient tolerating reduced-dose capecitabine well. CA 19-9 declining: 32 U/mL (down from 48 U/mL at surgery, 85 U/mL at end of neoadjuvant, 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis). Anastomotic fluid collection resolving. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome with capecitabine dose reduction. ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin exposure grade 1 (mild; stable, slowly improving). Weight stabilizing with PERT and dietary supplementation. Diabetes management: HbA1c 8.2% on insulin regimen. No evidence of disease recurrence on imaging or clinically. Cancer absent. Plan: Complete remaining 2 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Transition to surveillance after cycle 6.

At age 66 years, during cycles 5-6 of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, the patient developed grade 3 fatigue requiring a 1-week dose delay between cycles 5 and 6. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome persisted (stable). Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 72 x10^9/L) leading to gemcitabine dose reduction to 75% for cycle 6. No bleeding events. Patient completed cycle 6 with dose modifications. Total adjuvant course completed.

At age 66 years (approximately 7 months post-operatively, 1 month after completing adjuvant chemotherapy), the patient had end-of-treatment restaging CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest. Findings: Post-Whipple anatomy. No anastomotic fluid collection identified (resolved). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. No enlarged lymph nodes. Surgical bed: expected post-operative fibrosis without mass lesion or nodular enhancement suspicious for recurrence. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules or masses. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Complete resolution of post-operative fluid collection. CA 19-9: 21 U/mL (within normal range <37 U/mL). CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (normalized from 8.4 at diagnosis).

At age 66 years, end-of-treatment assessment following completion of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Patient has completed full multimodality treatment course: 8 cycles neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX, consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy with capecitabine), R0 Whipple procedure (ypT2N0M0, CAP score 2), and 6 cycles adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. CA 19-9 now 21 U/mL (normal). No radiographic evidence of disease. ECOG performance status 1. Ongoing issues: mild residual peripheral neuropathy (grade 1, stable), pancreatic exocrine insufficiency managed with PERT, post-Whipple insulin-dependent diabetes. Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant noted; counseled on implications for family members. Transition to surveillance: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and CA 19-9 every 3-4 months for first 2 years, then every 6 months for years 3-5. Oncology follow-up every 3 months. Referred to cancer survivorship program. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Restaging Assessment — After 4 Cycles Neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), pancreatic head and uncinate process, presenting today for restaging assessment and clinical review after completion of 4 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) in the neoadjuvant setting.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was originally diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC of the pancreatic head and uncinate process approximately 3 months ago. At diagnosis, cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head with tumor contact along the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) encompassing greater than 180 degrees of circumference and direct abutment of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with partial luminal compromise. One peripancreatic lymph node measuring 1.2 cm was suspicious for involvement. PET-CT confirmed regional nodal FDG avidity and no distant metastases. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal adenocarcinoma (grade 2). Staging: T4 N1 M0, stage III per AJCC 8th edition. Germline ATM pathogenic variant (heterozygous, p.V2716fs frameshift) was identified on reflex germline testing prompted by the somatic NGS findings; BRCA2 splice site variant detected only at low VAF on tissue NGS was not confirmed on germline testing. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL. CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, the consensus recommendation was for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with mFOLFIRINOX given good performance status, adequate end-organ function, and the goal of maximizing the chance of margin-negative (R0) resection by downsizing the tumor and its vascular involvement prior to surgical consideration. She was started on mFOLFIRINOX — oxali 85 mg/m2, iri 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced at cycle start from the standard 180 mg/m2 given her age of 66 and underlying diabetic neuropathy risk), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5FU (5-FU) 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion without bolus 5-FU. Pegfilgrastim was administered after each cycle. She tolerated cycle 1 well.

During cycle 2, she developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral distal paresthesias of hands and feet, classic cold-sensitivity typical of oxali), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 diarrhea. Loperamide was initiated for diarrhea management. Given the onset and severity of sensory neuropathy, oxali was dose-reduced by 20% to 68 mg/m2 for subsequent cycles. Camptosar was maintained at 150 mg/m2. No febrile neutropenia occurred. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 was 620 U/mL, already representing a 50% decline from baseline.

She presents today for review of restaging CT imaging performed after completion of cycle 4 and for clinical and laboratory reassessment to determine how to proceed.

Overall, she reports feeling reasonably well given the circumstances. She notes continued mild paresthesias in her fingertips and toes, which she rates as a 3/10 in terms of bother and not significantly impacting fine motor tasks or ambulation. She continues to use loperamide as needed for intermittent loose stools, approximately 2-3 loose bowel movements per day, down from a peak of 4-5 at cycle 2. She reports moderate fatigue — she is able to perform her usual light household activities but tires more quickly than her baseline. She denies nausea or vomiting between cycles. She reports a decreased appetite but has maintained her weight approximately at baseline. She denies new abdominal pain, jaundice, or change in urine color. No fevers or chills. She endorses ongoing cold sensitivity in her hands.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue (grade 2), stable appetite, no significant weight loss beyond expected, no fevers, no night sweats.
Neurologic: Bilateral distal sensory paresthesias, hands and feet, consistent with grade 2 peripheral neuropathy, stable since dose reduction of oxali after cycle 2. No motor weakness, no gait instability, no difficulty with buttoning or fine motor tasks.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day, grade 1-2), improved from nadir with loperamide. No nausea or vomiting. No melena, hematochezia. No worsening abdominal pain. Mild early satiety.
Skin: No significant changes. No rash.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias or myalgias beyond baseline.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no edema.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath, no cough.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. Endorses polyuria, consistent with worsened glycemic control.
Endocrine: Patient and her primary care physician have noted some worsening of blood sugar control during chemotherapy; this is being addressed with her primary care provider.
Psychiatric: Coping reasonably well. Strong family support. Has engaged with the oncology social worker. Reports some anxiety about restaging results, as expected.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg; Heart rate 76 bpm, regular; Temperature 37.0°C; Respiratory rate 16/min; Oxygen saturation 98% on room air; Weight 59.8 kg (baseline 61.2 kg at diagnosis — approximately 1.4 kg below baseline).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing. No acute distress. Appears age-stated. Mild fatigue evident but cooperative and communicative.
Head / Eyes / Ears / Nose / Throat: No icterus noted. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild residual fullness to deep palpation in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, not frankly tender. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Diminished sensation to light touch in bilateral digits (fingertips and toes). Vibration sense mildly reduced at the great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. Strength 5/5 throughout all four extremities. Gait steady; no ataxia.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Fingertips with mild subjective dysesthesia on exam; no trophic changes.
Skin: No jaundice. No rash or hand-foot changes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA 19-9: 310 U/mL (markedly decreased from 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis, from 620 U/mL after cycle 2; representing a 75% decline from the diagnostic baseline)
CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (improved from 8.4 ng/mL at diagnosis)
CBC: WBC 4.8 x10^9/L; ANC 2.7 x10^9/L; Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, consistent with chemotherapy-related); Platelets 188 x10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L; Potassium 3.9 mEq/L; Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL; BUN 14 mg/dL; Glucose 178 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known diabetes); Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL; Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL; AST 28 U/L; ALT 31 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase 94 U/L; Albumin 3.6 g/dL
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; supplementation recommended)
HbA1c (recent): 8.6% (suboptimally controlled; discussed with patient; to follow up with PCP for insulin adjustment)
INR: 1.1; PTT: 29 seconds

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol) was performed after cycle 4 of mFOLFIRINOX. Reviewed in detail with the radiology team.

Key findings: The primary pancreatic head/uncinate mass has clearly decreased in size, now measuring 3.1 x 2.5 cm, down from 3.8 x 3.2 cm at diagnosis. This represents a meaningful response — approximately a 22% decrease in the sum of longest diameters. The overall bulk of the tumor has softened, with the desmoplastic halo around the mass appearing somewhat less dense and confluent. Most importantly from a surgical standpoint, the degree of SMA contact has improved substantially: the tumor now contacts the SMA over approximately 90 to 120 degrees of circumference, down from greater than 180 degrees at diagnosis. This shift toward the 90-degree threshold is clinically very encouraging and suggests potential resectability may be achievable with continued treatment. The SMV demonstrates residual soft tissue contact, but the luminal caliber has improved and there is no longer meaningful luminal compromise. The previously suspicious peripancreatic lymph node has decreased to 0.7 cm and is no longer clearly abnormal. The main pancreatic duct dilation has partially improved (4 mm, down from 6 mm). No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants or ascites. No pulmonary nodules on the lower chest cuts. Overall, this scan documents a partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS — RESPONDING TO NEOADJUVANT mFOLFIRINOX

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is showing an excellent and encouraging response to neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX after 4 cycles. The CA 19-9 trajectory is particularly gratifying — a 75% decline from 1,247 to 310 U/mL is a strong marker of systemic disease response and favorable biology. This, combined with the clear reduction in tumor dimensions and significant improvement in SMA contact on cross-sectional imaging, indicates that the neoadjuvant approach is working. The SMA now appears to be contacted over roughly 90-120 degrees — still borderline, but meaningfully improved from the >180 degrees at presentation that rendered her initially unresectable by conventional criteria.

Plan: I had a detailed discussion with the patient today regarding these results. She was pleased and cautiously optimistic upon hearing about the response. We discussed that while these are encouraging signs, the tumor has not yet reached a point where the surgical team would feel confident proceeding directly to the operating room, and continued treatment is advisable.

Plan is to continue mFOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles (cycles 5 through 8), maintaining the dose modifications already in place (Eloxatin 68 mg/m2, Camptosar 150 mg/m2, no bolus 5-FU). After completion of 8 total cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, we will obtain repeat restaging imaging and multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment for surgical candidacy. The ultimate goal remains R0 resection if the vascular anatomy permits.

2. Eloxatin-RELATED PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY — GRADE 2, STABLE

Her grade 2 neuropathy has remained stable following the 20% oxali dose reduction after cycle 2. She tolerates her current activities of daily living and ambulation without difficulty. We discussed that cumulative oxali exposure remains a concern — ongoing monitoring is essential. If neuropathy worsens to grade 3 or becomes functionally disabling, we will need to consider discontinuing oxali and converting to a modified regimen. For now, plan is to continue with close monitoring at each cycle visit. She understands the importance of reporting any new or worsening symptoms. Magnesium supplementation prescribed given low-normal level (magnesium oxide 400 mg daily).

3. DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, IMPROVED

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with stage III borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma who has completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX with an impressive biochemical and radiographic response. Her CA 19-9 has declined 93% from a peak of 1,247 U/mL to the current 85 U/mL — this degree of decline is a favorable prognostic indicator and likely reflects substantial tumor burden reduction. Her tumor has shrunk from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm, and the critical SMA relationship has improved from abutment exceeding 180 degrees to what now appears to be approximately 90 degrees or less. Performance status remains ECOG 1.

This case was discussed in detail at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board meeting this week, attended by hepatobiliary surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

Key discussion points from tumor board:

1. SURGICAL RESECTABILITY: The surgical oncology team reviewed the most recent imaging and noted that while the response to mFOLFIRINOX has been gratifying, residual SMA contact — even though reduced to approximately 90 degrees — remains a concern for achieving a confidently negative retroperitoneal (SMA) margin at the time of Whipple. The surgeon's view is that the patient is potentially resectable, but proceeding directly to surgery at this point carries a meaningful risk of a close or positive posterior/SMA margin given the residual tumor-vessel contact. The team is not comfortable proceeding straight to surgery without further local treatment.

2. CONSOLIDATIVE CHEMORADIATION: The consensus recommendation of the multidisciplinary team is to proceed with consolidative neoadjuvant chemoradiation prior to surgical reassessment. The rationale includes: (a) to sterilize the SMA margin and maximize the chance of R0 resection, (b) to continue putting pressure on what has been a chemotherapy-responsive tumor, and (c) to provide definitive local treatment in the event microscopic disease persists at the vascular margin. The radiation oncology team discussed two approaches: stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT, typically 33-40 Gy in 5 fractions) versus conventionally fractionated chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine). After considerable discussion, the tumor board consensus favored conventional long-course chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine for this patient, given the degree of residual vascular involvement and the desire for maximal local sterilization effect at the SMA margin. The radiation oncology team will perform detailed treatment planning using IMRT with daily IGRT; fiducial markers will be placed prior to simulation.

3. TIMING: After completion of chemoradiation (approximately 5.5 weeks of daily treatment), the patient will have a rest period of 6-8 weeks before restaging imaging. Surgical reassessment will occur at that restaging visit, with Whipple procedure planned if the imaging and clinical picture support resectability.

4. PLAN SUMMARY:
   - Refer to radiation oncology for simulation planning and fiducial marker placement
   - Concurrent capecitabine (oral, 825 mg/m2 twice daily on days of radiation, approximately standard for this approach)
   - Total radiotherapy: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions over 5.5 weeks
   - No additional systemic chemotherapy during this chemoradiation phase
   - Restaging multiphasic CT after a 6-8 week break post-chemoradiation
   - Multidisciplinary reassessment for surgery following restaging

5. TOXICITY CONSIDERATIONS: I discussed with the patient that chemoradiation can cause nausea, fatigue, radiation gastritis, and potential skin reactions. Given her pre-existing oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (currently grade 1-2), capecitabine should be used with awareness of potential hand-foot syndrome — this will be monitored closely. Antiemetic prophylaxis and proton pump inhibitor prophylaxis will be prescribed. Nutritional support and dietitian involvement are strongly recommended given her current mild malnutrition (albumin 3.5 g/dL, weight loss of approximately 5 kg from baseline).

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Her glucose control remains suboptimal (HbA1c 8.0%, fasting glucose ~150 mg/dL). This is at least partly attributable to the pancreatic head tumor's impact on endocrine function in addition to her pre-existing type 2 diabetes. Endocrinology co-management will continue throughout chemoradiation. Corticosteroid use will be minimized.

7. ATM GERMLINE VARIANT: The patient has been counseled regarding her germline ATM p.V2716fs pathogenic variant. In the current setting, there is no approved targeted therapy for ATM-mutant pancreatic cancer outside of clinical trials. PARP inhibitors are predominantly approved in the BRCA context (confirmed germline BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant), which she does not have. However, she should be informed about clinical trial options that may become relevant in the future, including trials targeting the DNA damage response pathway. For now, management follows standard of care.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND RECOVERY: ECOG 1. The patient reports improved energy over the past 2-3 weeks since completing cycle 8. She is willing and motivated to proceed with chemoradiation. I anticipate she will tolerate the treatment reasonably well given her overall functional status and motivational reserve.

I spent significant time discussing the treatment plan with Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her family today. She understands the goals — maximizing the likelihood of successful and margin-negative surgery — and accepts the recommendation for chemoradiation prior to surgical resection. All questions were answered. She is agreeable to proceeding.

On current examination, the mass measures approximately 2.3 x 1.9 cm in the axial plane (previously 2.6 x 2.1 cm at the post-8-cycle mFOLFIRINOX scan, 3.1 x 2.5 cm at mid-treatment, and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at baseline). This represents continued, progressive dimensional reduction from prior examinations, consistent with an ongoing treatment response. The lesion demonstrates only modest enhancement relative to surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on pancreatic parenchymal phase images, with a persistent hypodense appearance. The surrounding desmoplastic halo remains present but appears less prominent and less extensive than on prior studies. There is no new internal necrosis or gas to suggest abscess formation.

Vascular Relationships — Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA):
At baseline, the primary tumor contacted the SMA over more than 180 degrees of circumference, the defining criterion for borderline/locally advanced disease. On the post-4-cycle scan, this contact had reduced to approximately 90-120 degrees. On the post-8-cycle scan, this was estimated at approximately 90 degrees or less. On the current examination, the soft tissue at the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA now contacts the artery over an estimated arc of approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. The SMA wall itself appears smooth and without encasement. The fat plane between the residual tumor mass and the right lateral SMA wall is extremely narrow but is appreciable on thin-section reconstructions, particularly on delayed phase imaging. There is no gross SMA infiltration or irregular narrowing of the arterial lumen to suggest intimal involvement.

Vascular Relationships — Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Confluence:
Previous examinations demonstrated direct tumor contact with the SMV with mild luminal narrowing. Following the completion of chemoradiation, the SMV now demonstrates a patent lumen with maintained caliber throughout its course. There is minimal residual soft tissue thickening along the right lateral wall of the SMV at the prior site of most intimate contact; however, this does not result in any appreciable luminal compromise. The portal confluence is patent. The superior mesenteric vein flows smoothly into the portal vein without web, thrombus, or occlusion. No cavernous transformation is noted. The celiac axis and common hepatic artery are patent and unremarkable.

Main Pancreatic Duct:
The main pancreatic duct diameter has normalized compared to baseline (where it measured 6 mm). Current diameter is approximately 3 mm in the body of the pancreas upstream of the mass, which represents near-complete normalization of the previously noted obstructive dilation. This is likely a reflection of the diminished obstructive burden of the residual mass.

Biliary System:
The intrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct is not discretely identified distal to the pancreatic head given the known tumor, consistent with prior course. No biliary stent is present. No pneumobilia. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without gallstones or wall thickening.

Regional and Distant Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, periportal, celiac, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric regions. The previously noted peripancreatic lymph node of concern (1.2 cm at baseline, 0.7 cm at mid-treatment, approximately 0.5 cm at post-8-cycle scan) is no longer clearly seen as a discrete nodal structure; there is a very small soft tissue density in this region measuring approximately 4 mm, indeterminate in significance but not appreciably enlarged. No para-aortic or iliac nodal enlargement.

The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Liver parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement throughout all phases. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypodense, hypervascular, or rim-enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic deposits. Previously noted absence of hepatic metastases is maintained. No biliary ductal dilation within the liver. Hepatic veins and portal venous system are patent.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneously enhancing. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal mass or nodule identified.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys demonstrate normal size, shape, and cortical thickness with prompt, symmetric contrast enhancement. No solid renal masses. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding. A 5 mm simple cortical cyst is noted in the left kidney, unchanged from prior examination.

Stomach and Bowel:
The stomach is unremarkable. Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness. The large bowel is normal in caliber. No obstruction. No wall thickening or mucosal irregularity to suggest radiation enteritis or colitis. No pneumatosis. No free air.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
The peritoneal surfaces are smooth. There is no peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or serosal implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The mesentery is homogeneous without soft tissue stranding beyond the expected post-treatment changes in the peripancreatic region. No ascites.

Abdominal Aorta and Vasculature:
The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification. No aneurysmal dilation. Celiac axis, splenic artery, common hepatic artery, and SMA origins appear patent. The inferior vena cava is patent throughout.

Bone Windows (Abdomen and Pelvis):
No lytic, sclerotic, or mixed osseous lesions in the thoracic or lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones to suggest metastatic disease. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine at L3-4 and L4-5 are noted, unchanged.

Pelvis:
The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable given patient's age. Urinary bladder is normally distended and smooth-walled. No pelvic free fluid. No iliac or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

IMPRESSION:

1. CONTINUED DIMENSIONAL REDUCTION OF PANCREATIC HEAD/UNCINATE MASS following completion of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) and consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy with concurrent cape). The lesion now measures approximately 2.3 x 1.9 cm, compared to 2.6 x 2.1 cm on the most recent prior scan and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at initial presentation. The cumulative reduction from baseline is substantial, consistent with meaningful locoregional treatment effect.

2. MARKEDLY IMPROVED VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS. Contact of the residual mass with the superior mesenteric artery has diminished considerably across serial examinations and is currently estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of arc. The previously noted SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees has undergone sustained, progressive reduction. An extremely narrow fat plane is now perceptible adjacent to the right lateral SMA wall on thin reconstructions. The SMV is fully patent without luminal compromise; only minimal residual soft tissue thickening persists at the prior SMV contact site. Overall vascular anatomy now appears substantially more favorable than at initial staging.

3. NO EVIDENCE OF DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE. No hepatic lesions, no pulmonary nodules, no peritoneal implants, no osseous lesions, and no pathologically enlarged lymphadenopathy identified.

4. NEAR-COMPLETE RESOLUTION OF PANCREATIC DUCT DILATION. Main pancreatic duct now measures approximately 3 mm, essentially normalized compared to baseline (6 mm), indicating reduced obstructive effect from the treated primary lesion.

5. The overall radiographic picture is one of a meaningful and ongoing treatment response. The residual soft tissue density at the primary tumor site may represent viable residual tumor, treatment-related fibrosis, or a combination of both — distinction cannot be made on imaging alone and will require histopathologic assessment at the time of surgical resection.

These findings support multidisciplinary surgical reassessment for potential pancreaticoduodenectomy. The vascular anatomy as currently depicted may permit resection with intent to achieve negative margins, though final determination of technical resectability remains the domain of the surgical team with intraoperative evaluation.

Correlation with serum CA 19-9 and multidisciplinary tumor board review is advised.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma who presents for pre-operative evaluation and clearance for planned pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). She has completed approximately 8 months of multimodality neoadjuvant therapy, including 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX followed by consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation with concurrent cape (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions), and now presents for surgical reassessment and final pre-operative planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired schoolteacher with a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who initially presented approximately 8 months ago with new-onset worsening of her diabetes and nonspecific epigastric discomfort. Workup at that time revealed a hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas measuring 3.8 x 3.2 cm on multiphasic pancreatic protocol CT. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (grade 2, infiltrating duct carcinoma). Staging workup including PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease with one FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node and no evidence of distant metastases. CA 19-9 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. CEA was mildly elevated at 8.4 ng/mL. NGS on the tumor demonstrated KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and additional alterations; MSI-stable, TMB-low. Germline testing confirmed a heterozygous ATM pathogenic frameshift variant; BRCA2 variant was determined to be somatic-only on germline testing.

The case was reviewed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board at the time of diagnosis. Given SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and direct SMV involvement with partial luminal narrowing, the tumor was deemed borderline resectable, and immediate surgery was not felt to offer an adequate chance of R0 resection. A neoadjuvant approach was recommended with the intent of converting the patient to resectable disease.

She proceeded with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX: Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 (reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral neuropathy), iri 150 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours (bolus 5-FU omitted). She completed 8 cycles in total. Cycle 6 required a 1-week dose delay due to grade 3 fatigue. Serial CA 19-9 monitoring demonstrated a robust decline: 1,247 → 620 (after cycle 2) → 310 (after cycle 4) → 85 U/mL (after cycle 8), representing a 93% reduction from baseline. Mid-treatment restaging CT after cycle 4 showed the primary tumor shrinking from 3.8 x 3.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.5 cm with improved SMA contact, decreased from >180 degrees to approximately 90-120 degrees. After all 8 cycles, the mass had further reduced to 2.6 x 2.1 cm with SMA contact at approximately 90 degrees or less.

Following completion of mFOLFIRINOX, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended consolidative chemoradiation given residual borderline SMA contact. She received conventionally fractionated IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) with concurrent Xeloda 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days, completed approximately 2 months ago. She tolerated chemoradiation with grade 2 nausea, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 radiation gastritis (managed with escalated PPI), and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome from Xeloda. She lost approximately 3 kg during the chemoradiation course.

Restaging CT obtained approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation showed further reduction in the primary mass to 2.3 x 1.9 cm. SMA contact is now estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. SMV appears patent with only minimal soft tissue thickening at the prior contact site. No new hepatic lesions, no adenopathy, no peritoneal disease. CA 19-9 at that time was 72 U/mL. The case was re-presented to the multidisciplinary tumor board, which confirmed resectability based on this favorable radiographic response and tumor marker trend. Surgical oncology was asked to evaluate for pancreaticoduodenectomy.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports that she has been recovering well from chemoradiation. Her epigastric discomfort has improved substantially since radiation. She notes mild persistent fatigue — much improved from the nadir during chemotherapy — which she rates as 3/10 in severity. She reports no new abdominal pain, no jaundice, no pruritus, no change in stool color or consistency (she has been monitoring given the prior biliary dilation). She has maintained her weight over the past 4-6 weeks after the losses during chemoradiation. Her appetite is fair. She is ambulatory without difficulty and is independent in all activities of daily living. She lives alone but has a daughter and son-in-law who will be available to provide post-operative support and who are aware of the planned surgery.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4/CDKN2A deleted, MSS, germline ATM pathogenic variant) who has had an excellent response to a prolonged and well-tolerated neoadjuvant course. She has gone from a tumor that was felt to be unresectable at presentation — with SMA encasement exceeding 180 degrees — to one that now appears favorably positioned for curative-intent resection. This is a patient who has done everything right: tolerated 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX with dose modifications as expected, completed the full course of chemoradiation, and has demonstrated a sustained, progressive decline in CA 19-9 from 1,247 down to 72 U/mL. She is now ready for definitive surgical resection.

The multidisciplinary tumor board has reviewed the most recent restaging and has endorsed proceeding with surgery. I concur with this assessment. There is no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Performance status is ECOG 1. She is clinically well-nourished enough for surgery, though we will take extra precautions given mild pre-operative malnutrition (albumin 3.4).

1. Planned operation: Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD/Whipple procedure) with curative intent. Classic versus pylorus-preserving technique was discussed with the patient; pylorus preservation is preferred given comparable oncologic outcomes and potential functional benefits. Standard regional lymphadenectomy will be performed. En bloc resection of the pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, proximal jejunum (approximately 10-15 cm), gallbladder, distal common bile duct, and the second portion of the duodenum/ampullary region. Reconstruction via pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa preferred), hepaticojejunostomy, and duodenojejunostomy in standard fashion.

2. Vascular assessment: Based on current imaging, venous reconstruction of the SMV/portal vein is not anticipated to be necessary. However, the surgical team will have vascular surgery backup availability and the necessary tools for patch repair or segmental reconstruction should intraoperative findings suggest closer involvement than appreciated on imaging. SMA proximity of approximately 60-70 degrees should be separable with careful dissection; R0 intent at the SMA margin is critical, and frozen section of this margin will be sent intraoperatively.

3. Pre-operative optimization:
   - Nutritional support: Oral nutritional supplementation (Ensure or equivalent, two servings daily) recommended from now until day before surgery. Albumin 3.4 g/dL is mildly low but acceptable; a targeted 2-3 week pre-op nutritional window is appropriate and feasible within our surgical timeline. Patient counseled on high-protein diet.
   - Glycemic management: Endocrinology informed of surgical date. Current insulin regimen (Lantus 18 units at bedtime + lispro sliding scale) will be adjusted per our institutional perioperative diabetes protocol. Metformin held 48 hours pre-operatively and renal function rechecked prior. Target perioperative blood glucose 140-180 mg/dL. Patient and her daughter educated on monitoring and sick-day rules.
   - Cardiopulmonary clearance: Patient has no significant cardiac history. Functional capacity is adequate by history (can climb one flight of stairs without difficulty, walks 30 minutes daily). 12-lead ECG ordered; echocardiogram not felt to be necessary at baseline but will order if ECG is abnormal. Formal cardiology clearance not required per our institutional criteria; primary care physician notified.
   - Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation: Appointment scheduled. Will flag Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, mild anemia, controlled diabetes, and history of radiation-induced gastritis (relevant for airway management and post-operative pain management strategy).
   - Bowel prep: Mechanical bowel prep with polyethylene glycol the day before surgery plus oral antibiotics (neomycin and erythromycin per our standard protocol).
   - Peri-operative antibiotics: Cefoxitin IV at induction; adjusted for allergy status (NKDA — no modification required).
   - DVT prophylaxis: Enoxaparin post-operatively (timing per anesthesia and surgeon agreement given planned epidural); pneumatic compression devices intraoperatively.
   - Medications: Continue amlodipine perioperatively. Hold metformin 48 hours pre-op. Insulin management as above. PPI (omeprazole) continued; will transition to IV pantoprazole perioperatively.

4. Informed consent: Extensive discussion held with Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her daughter today regarding the planned procedure. We reviewed the goals of surgery (curative intent, removal of the cancer with negative margins), the general risks of pancreaticoduodenectomy — including but not limited to: pancreatic leak/fistula (approximately 10-20% rate of biochemically relevant fistula), delayed gastric emptying, bile leak, wound infection, intra-abdominal abscess, hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, pneumonia, and general anesthetic risks — as well as longer-term consequences including pancreatic exocrine insufficiency requiring enzyme replacement and high likelihood of post-operative endocrine insufficiency (new or worsening diabetes). We discussed that given her pre-existing diabetes and the degree of neoadjuvant treatment, including prior chemoradiation (which affects tissue healing), her risk for pancreatic fistula and wound complications may be modestly elevated above baseline. We also discussed that intraoperative findings might reveal disease that precludes resection (approximately 10-15% of planned pancreatectomies are aborted, though this is lower in patients who have had extensive neoadjuvant therapy and favorable pre-operative imaging). She verbalized understanding and signed written informed consent. She was given the opportunity to ask questions; she and her daughter asked thoughtful questions about recovery time and return to activity, which were answered in detail.

5. Surgeon's assessment of resectability: Based on clinical, laboratory, and radiographic assessment, I believe Ms. Osei-Bonsu is technically resectable with a high likelihood of achieving an R0 resection. The response she has demonstrated — both radiographically and with respect to tumor markers — is one of the better responses we see in the neoadjuvant pancreatic cancer setting. The decline in CA 19-9 by over 94% and the clear shrinkage and regression of vascular contact speak to meaningful treatment effect. I am optimistic about this case but have counseled the patient that pathologic findings at the time of resection will ultimately determine the next steps in her care.

6. Scheduling: Surgery has been scheduled. Pre-operative blood work (CBC, CMP, coagulation, type and screen, HbA1c, repeat CA 19-9) to be drawn on the morning of pre-anesthesia evaluation. Patient will be admitted the morning of surgery (same-day admission).

7. Follow-up: Patient will be seen by the hepatobiliary surgical team in clinic post-operatively. Medical oncology has been notified of planned surgical date and will reassess for adjuvant therapy planning after adequate post-operative recovery, typically 8-12 weeks post-operatively, contingent on final pathology.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Multiple sections through the pancreatic head and uncinate process demonstrate a moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive treatment-related changes. Residual viable carcinoma is present in the form of irregular, angulated glands and small nests of epithelial cells embedded within a densely fibrotic, paucicellular desmoplastic stroma. The neoplastic glands exhibit moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, irregular luminal contours, and intraluminal dirty necrosis in scattered foci. Mitotic figures are infrequent (1-2 per 10 high-power fields) in areas of residual viable tumor.

Surrounding the residual viable tumor, extensive treatment effect is identified: large zones of acellular hyalinized fibrosis, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, foamy histiocytes, cholesterol cleft foreign body giant cell reaction, scattered lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, and focal acellular mucin pools. Ghost outlines of necrotic glands are present. These findings account for approximately 65-75% of the original estimated tumor bed and are consistent with substantial but incomplete tumor regression, designated CAP Tumor Regression Score 2.

Perineural invasion is present, with carcinoma cells identified encircling and permeating small to medium-sized nerves in 3 separate foci within the tumor and at the periphery of the treatment-effect zone. Lymphovascular invasion is present in one focus at the leading edge of the residual viable carcinoma, confirmed by D2-40 immunohistochemistry.

The posterior (SMA) inked margin is free of carcinoma at 0.1 cm clearance, with acellular fibrous tissue and scattered macrophages at the margin interface — no viable carcinoma cells are present at the inked surface. The pancreatic neck margin shows chronic pancreatitis and PanIN1 without carcinoma. Remaining margins are widely negative.

All 22 lymph nodes are negative for metastatic carcinoma. Two peripancreatic nodes demonstrate residual fibrous nodules with hemosiderin, consistent with complete regression of prior nodal metastases.

Background pancreas shows diffuse chronic obstructive pancreatitis with acinar atrophy, ductal dilatation, periductal fibrosis, and islet cell clustering. PanIN-1 and PanIN-2 lesions are present in non-involved pancreatic ducts remote from the primary tumor. No PanIN-3 (carcinoma in situ) is identified.

The duodenal mucosa, gallbladder, common bile duct, and proximal jejunal segment show no malignancy.

DIAGNOSTIC COMMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This pancreaticoduodenectomy specimen demonstrates a moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with a moderate treatment response (CAP regression score 2) following neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX and chemoradiation. Residual viable carcinoma accounts for approximately 25-35% of the original tumor bed. All surgical margins are negative (R0 resection). Regional lymph nodes are negative (0/22; ypN0), with two nodes demonstrating histologic evidence of complete regression of prior nodal metastases — consistent with the observed decline in CA 19-9 and radiographic lymph node response during neoadjuvant therapy. The close posterior (SMA) margin of 0.1 cm, while negative, should be considered in the context of risk for local recurrence.

Presence of perineural invasion and focal lymphovascular invasion in residual viable tumor are adverse histopathologic features in the post-neoadjuvant setting. SMAD4 loss confirmed by IHC (concordant with prior biopsy and genomic data) and aberrant p53 pattern are consistent with the previously characterized molecular profile. MMR protein expression is intact (all four proteins retained), supporting prior MSS determination.

Findings reported to the referring surgical oncology team.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Microscopic examination of the formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded core biopsy material reveals multiple histologic sections of pancreatic parenchyma infiltrated by an invasive epithelial neoplasm. The sections are of adequate diagnostic quality with minimal crush artifact.

The tumor is characterized by irregular, angulated, and haphazardly arranged glandular structures composed of cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells with moderate to abundant amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear pleomorphism is moderate: nuclei are enlarged, vesicular, and irregular with variably prominent nucleoli. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios are increased. Mitotic figures are identified, including occasional atypical mitoses. Intraluminal mucin is present in a subset of glandular profiles. The tumor glands show incomplete luminal formation and focal cribriforming in areas. Infiltration is irregular and jagged at the advancing front, with individual tumor cells and small abortive glands extending into the surrounding fibrous stroma.

Background stroma is markedly desmoplastic, composed of dense, hypocellular collagenous fibrous tissue with scattered activated stellate cells and a sparse chronic inflammatory infiltrate predominantly composed of lymphocytes and macrophages. This desmoplastic reaction is a prominent and characteristic feature, constituting approximately 60-70% of the total tissue volume on the core biopsy sections.

No unequivocal perineural invasion is identified in the available sections; however, assessment is limited given the small sample size. Lymphovascular invasion is not identified in the tissue provided. No necrosis is identified.

Residual non-neoplastic pancreatic acini are present at the periphery of the core material and show reactive/atrophic changes, including loss of zymogen granules and lobular atrophy, consistent with obstructive pancreatitis. Islets of Langerhans are focally identified. No pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) is definitively identified in the limited sample, though focal ductal epithelial changes are present and cannot be fully characterized on biopsy.

The cell block (Block A2) shows similar findings with small clusters of malignant glandular epithelium embedded in fibrous stroma, confirming the core biopsy findings.

Histologic grade: Moderately differentiated (Grade 2 of 3) based on glandular architecture and degree of nuclear pleomorphism.

This moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma shows the typical constellation of morphologic features, including infiltrative glandular architecture, desmoplastic stroma, and moderate nuclear atypia. Pending molecular studies (comprehensive NGS panel) will further characterize the genomic landscape of this tumor and may identify actionable alterations or confirm germline testing recommendations.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the head and uncinate process who presents for her 6-week follow-up after pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD). She was discharged from the hospital approximately 4 weeks ago following a prolonged admission for a post-operative grade B pancreatic fistula. She is here to discuss pathology results in full, ongoing recovery, management of new post-surgical complications, and planning for adjuvant systemic therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC of the pancreatic head and uncinate process (cT4 N1 M0, Stage III, AJCC 8th edition) approximately nine months ago. Her presenting CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis, and her CEA was 8.4 ng/mL. Staging workup including multiphasic CT with pancreatic protocol and FDG PET-CT confirmed a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass with SMA contact exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and partial SMV occlusion. Histologic diagnosis was established by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy demonstrating moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma with SMAD4 loss. Comprehensive molecular profiling revealed KRAS G12V and TP53 R248W mutations, SMAD4 and CDKN2A homozygous deletions, and a germline ATM pathogenic frameshift variant (p.V2716fs, confirmed on germline testing). MSS/MSI-stable, TMB-low, PD-L1 CPS 2.

She was taken to multidisciplinary tumor board which recommended neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) given her ECOG 1 performance status and absence of distant metastatic disease. She completed 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (oxali 85 mg/m2 reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy; iri 150 mg/m2 per dose-reduced starting regimen; leucovorin 400 mg/m2; 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46-hour infusion without bolus). She had meaningful CA 19-9 decline to 85 U/mL and radiographic partial response with tumor reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm and SMA contact decreasing to approximately 90 degrees. She then underwent consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation — 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent cape 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days — which further reduced the primary lesion to 2.3 x 1.9 cm with SMA contact estimated at 60-70 degrees and an essentially patent SMV. CA 19-9 was 72 U/mL at that point, declining further to 48 U/mL by the time of surgery.

She underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy approximately 6 weeks ago. Intraoperative findings were favorable: the primary tumor was in the pancreatic head/uncinate, grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA; the SMV was dissected free without venous reconstruction; no peritoneal implants or hepatic metastases were encountered. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin and bile duct margin were negative. Reconstruction was via duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, and duodenojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss was 650 mL; one unit of packed red blood cells was transfused. A Jackson-Pratt drain was placed near the pancreaticojejunostomy.

Her post-operative course was complicated by a clinically relevant grade B pancreatic fistula (per ISGPF criteria): drain amylase peaked at approximately 4,800 U/L on post-operative day 7, drain output was 180 mL/day, and she had low-grade fever to 38.1°C without systemic sepsis. She was managed conservatively with bowel rest, total parenteral nutrition, and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneous TID. Her hospital stay was extended to 18 days. She was discharged home with the drain in situ and returned to the clinic approximately 3 weeks ago where the drain was removed once output had decreased to minimal levels and amylase had normalized — consistent with fistula resolution at approximately 5 weeks post-operatively.

Today at 6 weeks post-operatively she reports gradual improvement in energy levels, though she remains fatigued. She is tolerating a soft-to-regular diet but notes she has experienced loose, greasy stools with a foul odor since the drain was removed, consistent with steatorrhea. She reports approximately 3.5 kg of weight loss from her pre-operative weight and has had difficulty maintaining caloric intake due to early satiety and intermittent nausea. She has been checking her blood glucose at home and reporting significant hyperglycemia since the surgery, which the endocrinology team has already begun addressing. She has mild bilateral distal extremity paresthesias in the fingertips and toes, which she reports as slightly better than during chemotherapy. She denies abdominal pain, jaundice, acholic stools (separate from the steatorrhea), hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No fevers or chills since discharge. She is managing her surgical incision without difficulty.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue (moderate), weight loss of approximately 3.5 kg from pre-surgical baseline, decreased appetite with early satiety.

PATHOLOGY REVIEW (Surgical Specimen — Final Report Reviewed Today in Detail with Patient):
The final Whipple specimen pathology has been reviewed in its entirety with Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her family. Key findings discussed:
- Histology: Moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head and uncinate process
- Tumor size: 2.1 x 1.8 cm (significantly reduced from the pre-treatment 3.8 cm primary, reflecting treatment response)
- Treatment effect: CAP tumor regression grade 2 — moderate response; residual viable carcinoma present but with evident areas of fibrosis and necrosis attributable to neoadjuvant therapy. This is an intermediate response category and is consistent with her radiographic and serologic response.
- Surgical margins: All margins are NEGATIVE (R0 resection). This is the critical finding I discussed with patient at length. The pancreatic neck margin has 0.4 cm clearance, the posterior retroperitoneal/SMA margin is negative at 0.1 cm (close but genuinely negative), and the uncinate, bile duct, anterior, and distal margins are all negative.
- Lymph nodes: 0 out of 22 lymph nodes examined show metastatic involvement (ypN0). This is an outstanding result — all 22 sampled regional nodes are free of cancer.
- Pathologic stage after neoadjuvant therapy: ypT2 ypN0 cM0, Stage IIA (AJCC 8th edition). Downstaged from clinical T4 N1 to pathologic T2 N0.
- Lymphovascular invasion: focally present. Perineural invasion: present. These are noted but do not change management at this time.
- Background pathology: Chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) grades 1-2 in the non-neoplastic pancreatic parenchyma.

I emphasized to the patient that achieving an R0 resection with complete lymph node clearance after neoadjuvant therapy is the best possible surgical outcome for this diagnosis, and represents a meaningful accomplishment given the extent of disease at presentation. The significance of the close SMA margin was discussed; while negative at 0.1 cm, this margin was addressed by neoadjuvant therapy and chemoradiation, and no further local therapy is indicated at this time. The presence of residual perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion on pathology underscores the rationale for proceeding with adjuvant systemic therapy.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. POST-OPERATIVE RECOVERY FOLLOWING R0 WHIPPLE PROCEDURE (ypT2N0M0 PANCREATIC ADENOCARCINOMA):
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has achieved an excellent surgical result following a prolonged and intensive multimodality neoadjuvant treatment course. She is now approximately 6 weeks post-operatively, recovering appropriately, with resolution of her grade B pancreatic fistula. Her overall trajectory is encouraging: CA 19-9 of 48 U/mL at this visit — down from a pre-treatment peak of 1,247 U/mL — reflects the cumulative tumor burden reduction through neoadjuvant and surgical phases of treatment. I expect further normalization on subsequent checks once she is on adjuvant therapy and the post-operative period progresses. We will proceed with adjuvant therapy planning as outlined below.

2. PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY — INITIATION OF PERT:
Fecal elastase-1 is 148 mcg/g, confirming pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (EPI), a near-universal consequence of pancreaticoduodenectomy given resection of the pancreatic head. This explains her steatorrhea and likely contributes to her weight loss and nutritional deficit. I am initiating pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy today:
- Pancrelipase (Creon) 40,000 lipase units orally with each main meal and 20,000 lipase units with snacks. Advise patient to take with the first bite of food, not before or after. Enzymes must not be crushed.
- Dietary counseling: She should follow a low-fat diet transiently while enzymes are titrated, with gradual liberalization. Referral to our oncology dietitian placed today.
- Supplemental oral nutrition (Ensure Plus or equivalent) to continue as a caloric supplement between meals.
- Anticipate progressive improvement in stool consistency and weight stabilization within 3-4 weeks of PERT initiation.
- Vitamin supplementation: Increase vitamin D3 to 2,000 IU daily; continue B12 supplementation. Monitor fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) at next visit.

3. NEW-ONSET POST-PANCREATECTOMY INSULIN-DEPENDENT DIABETES MELLITUS (TYPE 3c):
Resection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process has caused what is effectively type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes, superimposed on her prior type 2 diabetes. Post-Whipple diabetes can be particularly brittle due to loss of both insulin-secreting beta cells and glucagon-secreting alpha cells, creating risk for hypoglycemia as well as hyperglycemia. Her endocrinology team is actively managing her insulin regimen. Today's HbA1c is 8.6% and fasting glucose is 187 mg/dL, indicating suboptimal control that needs to be addressed prior to chemotherapy initiation, as hyperglycemia worsens chemotherapy tolerability and infection risk. I have communicated directly with her endocrinologist. Metformin is being held, appropriately. Goals: FBG 100-140, post-prandial <200 prior to adjuvant therapy start. Continue close follow-up with endocrinology.

4. ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC CHEMOTHERAPY — PLANNING:
Given her pathologic staging (ypT2N0M0) and the presence of residual viable carcinoma with lymphovascular and perineural invasion, adjuvant systemic therapy is strongly indicated. The standard of care for adjuvant PDAC following resection — particularly after neoadjuvant therapy — is an area of evolving evidence. Given that she received neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX (oxali-containing), I discussed the following options:

a) gem plus cape (ESPAC-4 regimen): This is a well-validated adjuvant regimen with proven overall survival benefit over gem monotherapy. Given she has not received a gem-based backbone previously, her tumor would have a relatively chemotherapy-naive response to this combination. This is my preferred recommendation.

b) Gemzar monotherapy: A historical standard with less toxicity; considered in patients with poor performance status or significant comorbidities. Given her ECOG 1 status, I favor the combination.

c) Further mFOLFIRINOX/FOLFOX-based adjuvant therapy: Less favored given prior exposure, cumulative neuropathy burden, and limited data supporting this approach following neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX.

Recommendation: Proceed with adjuvant Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle, plus Xeloda 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21, for 6 cycles (approximately 6 months). Treatment intent is adjuvant with curative intent. Target start approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively (2-4 weeks from today) pending adequate recovery and glucose control. Pre-authorization submitted.

Pre-chemotherapy labs to be obtained in 2 weeks: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, CA 19-9. Repeat HbA1c. ECOG reassessment at that visit.

Cumulative Eloxatin neuropathy: Grade 1 currently (stable, slowly improving). The planned adjuvant regimen does not include Eloxatin, which is appropriate given her prior exposure. Xeloda re-exposure is planned; prior hand-foot syndrome was grade 1 during chemoradiation at 825 mg/m2 twice daily — the full adjuvant dose is higher and she should be counseled to monitor for HFS, use urea-based emollients on palms and soles, and report early symptoms.

5. GERMLINE ATM PATHOGENIC VARIANT:
Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant (p.V2716fs) confirmed on testing. Patient has been counseled on the implications for her own cancer risk (increased risk for other malignancy subtypes including possibly breast) and the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (50% chance of transmission to each first-degree relative). She has been referred to genetic counseling for ongoing management and family cascade testing. No immediate change to cancer management is indicated by this finding in the adjuvant/post-resection setting. PARP inhibitor use is generally not approved in ATM-mutated PDAC outside clinical trial context at this time.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with PDAC of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (originally stage III, T4N1M0, borderline resectable) who has responded remarkably well to her multimodality treatment program. She has completed 4 of 6 cycles of gemcitabine plus capecitabine. Her counts today are adequate for proceeding: ANC 2.1, platelets 148. Currently grade 1, well improved from the grade 2 episode that prompted dose reduction during cycles 1-3. On exam today: mild bilateral palmar erythema with superficial peeling, no blistering, no significant pain or functional impairment. No plantar involvement.
