Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (ypT2N0M0 after neoadjuvant therapy) who presents today for end-of-treatment assessment following completion of her sixth and final cycle of adjuvant gem plus cape. **HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was originally diagnosed approximately 17 months ago with a 3.8 cm borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, with tumor abutting the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over more than 180 degrees of circumference and directly contacting the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Her initial CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. She underwent EUS-guided biopsy confirming moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Comprehensive genomic profiling identified a KRAS G12V driver mutation, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. She was found to carry a germline ATM frameshift pathogenic variant on subsequent germline testing; a low-VAF BRCA2 splice variant detected on the tumor biopsy was not confirmed on germline testing and is considered somatic-only.

Given her good performance status (ECOG 1), intact organ function, and absence of distant metastatic disease, the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board recommended neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) with the intent of downsizing and potentially converting to resectability. She received 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (Eloxatin dose-reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bolus 5-FU omitted per modified regimen). Her CA 19-9 declined steadily — from 1,247 to 620 after cycle 2, to 310 after cycle 4, and to 85 U/mL after all 8 cycles. Restaging CT after 8 cycles showed the pancreatic mass had decreased from 3.8 x 3.2 cm to 2.6 x 2.1 cm, with the SMA contact reduced to approximately 90 degrees.

Given residual SMA abutment, the tumor board recommended consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation therapy: IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent Xeloda (825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days). She tolerated this reasonably well with grade 2 nausea, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 radiation-induced gastritis, and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, all managed without treatment interruption. A 3 kg weight loss occurred during radiation; nutritional counseling and oral supplementation were initiated.

Restaging CT approximately two months after completing chemoradiation showed further reduction to 2.3 x 1.9 cm with SMA contact estimated at 60-70 degrees, and the SMV appearing patent with only minimal residual soft tissue thickening. CA 19-9 at that point was 72 U/mL. The tumor board confirmed resectability, and she proceeded to pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis. Intraoperatively, the tumor was grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA; venous reconstruction of the SMV was not required. Final surgical pathology confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm, with extensive treatment effect (CAP tumor regression score 2). All surgical margins were negative — the posterior/SMA margin was the tightest at 0.1 cm but confirmed negative. Notably, all 22 sampled lymph nodes were free of malignancy (ypN0), yielding a final pathologic stage of ypT2N0M0.

Her post-operative course was complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula that developed by post-operative day 7, manifesting as elevated drain amylase, low-grade fever, and drain output of approximately 180 mL/day. This was managed conservatively with bowel rest, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and subcutaneous octreotide. The fistula gradually resolved over approximately five weeks without radiologic or operative intervention. She was discharged on post-operative day 18 and her drain was removed at her five-week post-operative visit. Her post-operative CA 19-9 nadir was 48 U/mL.

Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated approximately 10 weeks post-surgery with Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle plus cape per the ESPAC-4 regimen. Imaging at cycle 3 demonstrated resolving anastomotic fluid collection (down to 2.8 cm from 3.2 cm), no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal disease, and CA 19-9 of 32 U/mL. During cycles 5 and 6, she developed grade 3 fatigue requiring a one-week dose delay between cycles 5 and 6, and grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 72 x10^9/L) requiring Gemzar dose reduction to 75% for cycle 6. She completed cycle 6 with dose modifications; total adjuvant course completed.

We discussed the importance of regular follow-up given the high risk of recurrence in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma even after R0 resection with pathologic nodal clearance. The current planned surveillance schedule is CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast and CA 19-9 (with CEA) every 3 months for the first two years post-Whipple, then every 6 months for years three through five, and then annually thereafter. **5. Germline ATM pathogenic variant:**
Ms. Osei-Bonsu carries a germline heterozygous ATM frameshift pathogenic variant. She has been fully counseled regarding implications for her own cancer risk monitoring (pancreatic cancer surveillance programs for ATM carriers, increased lifetime risk for other malignancies including breast cancer) and implications for first-degree relatives. She has informed her adult children and two of them are seeking genetic counseling. Breast cancer surveillance: she is already due for her annual mammogram; referral placed. Continued coordination with the genetics team.

**6. Hypertension:**
Blood pressure well controlled at 128/76 mmHg on lisinopril and amlodipine. Continue current regimen. Primary care management.

**7. Nutritional rehabilitation and weight recovery:**
Weight is currently approximately 3.2 kg below her pre-illness baseline. This likely reflects a combination of the Whipple procedure sequelae, adjuvant chemotherapy, and reduced caloric intake over the past year. She is encouraged to gradually increase caloric density with the assistance of the dietitian. Will recheck weight and albumin at the next visit to assess trajectory.

**8. Psychosocial support and survivorship:**
I spent considerable time today discussing her experience, her fears about recurrence, and her goals going forward. She was referred to the cancer survivorship program and a pancreatic cancer survivor peer support group. She expressed interest in the Pancreatic Cancer Action Network (PanCAN) patient resources. She will begin working with a survivorship nurse navigator. We discussed the importance of regular physical activity as tolerated for overall health, well-being, and potentially cancer recurrence risk. Light aerobic activity (walking) encouraged and progressed as tolerated.

**9. Return to care:**
Return to oncology clinic in 3 months for surveillance CT and tumor markers. Patient was given my direct contact information and instructed to call with any concerning new symptoms — particularly new abdominal pain, jaundice, significant unintentional weight loss, or new back pain — before the next scheduled visit.

WHOLE-BODY FDG PET/CT SCAN

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with recently diagnosed pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma (EUS-guided biopsy confirmed, moderately differentiated). Multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis performed at the time of initial evaluation demonstrated a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate region with superior mesenteric artery (SMA) abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) involvement with partial luminal narrowing. One peripancreatic lymph node at 1.2 cm noted as suspicious. CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. PET/CT requested for comprehensive metabolic staging prior to initiation of treatment, to evaluate for occult distant metastatic disease not detected on diagnostic CT.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body integrated FDG PET/CT was performed following intravenous administration of 12.4 mCi (459 MBq) of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose. Patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to radiopharmaceutical injection. Serum glucose at the time of injection was 138 mg/dL (patient has known type 2 diabetes mellitus; no insulin administered within 4 hours of injection; exam proceeded per institutional protocol). Uptake period: 62 minutes. Imaging was performed from the vertex of the skull to mid-thigh using standard time-of-flight PET acquisition with low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic co-registration. Diagnostic quality CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous contrast as part of this examination (100 mL Omnipaque 350, power injected). Slice thickness: 3 mm reconstructed. Iterative reconstruction algorithm applied. All images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. SUV measurements represent standardized uptake values corrected for lean body mass (SUL) and body weight (SUVmax). Background liver SUVmean: 2.6.

FINDINGS:

HEAD, NECK, AND BRAIN:
No intracranial FDG-avid lesions. Normal cortical distribution of FDG. No hypermetabolic cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No parotid or submandibular gland uptake above physiologic background. No orbital or nasal cavity abnormalities. Thyroid gland demonstrates diffuse, mild, symmetric FDG uptake consistent with physiologic or low-grade thyroiditis pattern; no discrete hypermetabolic nodule identified. No focal osseous lesions in the calvarium or skull base.

CHEST:
Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses with abnormal FDG uptake. Several small bilateral pulmonary micronodules are noted on the CT component (largest 4 mm, right lower lobe), all below the threshold for reliable FDG avidity at this resolution; these are nonspecific and likely inflammatory or granulomatous in etiology. No lobar or segmental consolidation with abnormal metabolic activity. No pleural effusions. Pleura: No pleural nodularity or FDG-avid pleural implants.
Cardiomediastinum: Cardiac chambers demonstrate physiologic FDG distribution. No pericardial FDG avidity. Aorta and great vessels are normal in caliber and without abnormal uptake. Mediastinal contour unremarkable.
Lymph nodes: No enlarged or hypermetabolic mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes. Several small subcentimeter mediastinal lymph nodes are present (largest 7 mm in the right paratracheal station) and are non-FDG-avid, consistent with reactive or incidental nodes.
Bones (thoracic): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions with associated FDG uptake in the thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, or clavicles. No pathologic fractures.

UPPER ABDOMEN:
Pancreas - Primary Tumor: There is a conspicuous, intensely hypermetabolic soft tissue mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. This lesion demonstrates markedly elevated FDG avidity with an SUVmax of 8.9 (background liver SUVmean 2.6; lesion-to-liver ratio approximately 3.4). On the co-registered CT, the mass measures approximately 3.8 x 3.2 cm, appearing hypoenhancing relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma with an ill-defined margin and surrounding desmoplastic reaction. Extensive contact of the mass with the origin of the SMA is again confirmed on the CT component, with the tumor appearing to encircle greater than 180 degrees of the vessel circumference at the level of its takeoff. The SMV at the level of the pancreatic head appears narrowed at its confluence, with soft tissue abutment. Main pancreatic duct is moderately dilated, consistent with the previously described upstream ductal obstruction. The pancreatic body and tail show mild parenchymal atrophy upstream of the obstructing mass without abnormal FDG uptake to suggest secondary malignant involvement.

The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous without focal hepatic arterial or portal phase enhancement abnormality. No biliary ductal dilatation at the level of the hepatic hilum, though mild intrahepatic biliary prominence is present in the right hepatic lobe consistent with the known downstream biliary obstruction. The common bile duct measures 12 mm in maximal diameter on the CT component, consistent with prior evaluation.

Spleen: Normal size and homogeneous uptake. No splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses. No FDG-avid adrenal lesions. Small bilateral adrenal limbs are present without thickening or nodularity.
Kidneys: Normal bilateral renal uptake with physiologic tracer excretion into the collecting systems and ureters. No renal masses with abnormal FDG avidity. No hydronephrosis.
Stomach: Gastric wall demonstrates physiologic FDG uptake without focal hypermetabolic lesion. No evidence of gastric involvement.

LYMPH NODES - REGIONAL:
There is a single peripancreatic lymph node in the periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament region that demonstrates mildly elevated FDG uptake with an SUVmax of 4.2. On the CT component, this node measures approximately 1.2 cm in short axis and demonstrates soft tissue density without calcification. This degree of metabolic activity in this location in the setting of a known pancreatic head primary is suspicious for nodal metastasis, although reactive nodal enlargement cannot be entirely excluded at this level of avidity.
No additional regional lymph nodes in the celiac axis, superior mesenteric, para-aortic, or aortocaval chains demonstrate FDG avidity exceeding background or are enlarged beyond 1 cm in short axis on CT.

LOWER ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
Small and large bowel: Normal physiologic FDG distribution. No focal bowel wall uptake concerning for malignant involvement. No bowel wall thickening on CT.

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely hypermetabolic mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process (SUVmax 8.9), consistent with the known primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. 4. No peritoneal FDG avidity. No CT evidence of peritoneal implants or ascites. Peritoneal surface disease is not demonstrated.

5. No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or masses. The small bilateral micronodules are below the reliable threshold for PET assessment and are nonspecific.

6. No FDG-avid osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastases.

7. No FDG-avid distant lymphadenopathy in the mediastinum, retroperitoneum, or pelvis.

Overall, the findings are consistent with locoregional pancreatic adenocarcinoma with regional nodal involvement and no definitive evidence of distant metastatic disease on this whole-body FDG PET/CT examination. The primary tumor's metabolic profile is highly consistent with active, metabolically aggressive disease confined to the pancreatic head region and regional lymphatics at this time. These findings support further evaluation and treatment planning in the multidisciplinary oncologic setting.

EXAMINATION: Whole-body fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) positron emission tomography with concurrent low-dose non-contrast CT (PET/CT), skull base to mid-thigh.

The patient received 10.8 mCi (approximately 400 MBq) of F-18 FDG intravenously. Images were acquired approximately 62 minutes post-injection. Whole-body PET emission data were acquired in 3D mode and iteratively reconstructed with attenuation correction using the concurrent low-dose CT. The co-registered low-dose CT was obtained without intravenous or oral contrast and is used primarily for attenuation correction and anatomic localization; it is not a diagnostic-quality contrast-enhanced CT and should be interpreted in conjunction with the dedicated pancreatic protocol CT performed at this institution.

COMPARISON: Multiphasic CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol), performed at this institution.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No FDG-avid cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. No hypermetabolic lesions in the visualized brain parenchyma. No abnormal metabolic activity in the thyroid gland or salivary glands. Physiologic symmetric FDG activity is noted in the bilateral tonsils and palatine region.

CHEST:

UPPER ABDOMEN:

Pancreas: There is a markedly FDG-avid mass centered in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. The lesion demonstrates intense, heterogeneous tracer uptake with a measured peak SUVmax of 8.9 (mean SUV approximately 5.4). On co-registered CT, this corresponds to the known hypoenhancing mass measuring approximately 3.8 cm in greatest dimension, consistent with the primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma as characterized on the recent dedicated contrast-enhanced CT. No hypermetabolic satellite foci within the pancreatic body or tail are identified.

Regional lymph nodes: A single focus of moderately increased FDG uptake is identified in the peripancreatic/periportal region, anterior to the portal confluence, corresponding to a 1.2 cm lymph node seen on the dedicated contrast CT. SUVmax measures 4.2. This level of activity is clearly above background soft tissue uptake and is suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement. No other regional lymph nodes along the superior mesenteric artery distribution, celiac axis, para-aortic chain, or portocaval region demonstrate abnormal FDG avidity. PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants, serosal deposits, or focal areas of FDG hypermetabolism along the peritoneal surfaces, mesentery, or omentum to suggest carcinomatosis. No ascites identified on low-dose CT.

RETROPERITONEUM:

PELVIS:
No FDG-avid pelvic lymphadenopathy. No osseous lesions in the visualized pelvis. Uterus and ovaries: The uterus appears normal in size with no focal FDG uptake. Physiologic renal collecting system and bladder FDG activity without focal lesions.

MUSCULOSKELETAL / OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
No focal osseous FDG-avid lesions in the visualized axial or appendicular skeleton to suggest skeletal metastatic disease. No lytic or sclerotic foci on CT correlation suspicious for osseous involvement. Degenerative changes are noted in the thoracolumbar spine consistent with the patient's age, without associated hypermetabolic activity.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal FDG-avid soft tissue masses or subcutaneous lesions identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. INTENSELY FDG-AVID PRIMARY PANCREATIC MASS: Markedly hypermetabolic mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process (SUVmax 8.9), entirely consistent with the biopsy-confirmed pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The degree of metabolic activity reflects an aggressive, highly glycolytic tumor in keeping with the known pathologic diagnosis.

2. FDG-AVID REGIONAL LYMPH NODE: A single peripancreatic/periportal lymph node demonstrates moderately increased FDG uptake (SUVmax 4.2), consistent with nodal involvement and concordant with findings on prior contrast CT. No additional nodal stations demonstrate pathologic metabolic activity.

3. NO HEPATIC METASTASES: No FDG-avid hepatic lesions identified. The liver appears metabolically and morphologically normal on this examination.

4. NO PULMONARY METASTASES: No hypermetabolic pulmonary nodules or masses. The lungs appear clear.

5. NO PERITONEAL DISEASE: No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis or serosal implants by metabolic or CT criteria.

6. NO DISTANT ORGAN OR SKELETAL METASTASES: No hypermetabolic foci in the adrenal glands, bones, or other distant sites.

OVERALL IMPRESSION: FDG PET/CT is consistent with locoregional pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The dominant FDG-avid primary tumor in the pancreatic head (SUVmax 8.9) with a single suspicious peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2) represents regional disease without detectable distant metastatic spread. The absence of occult hepatic, peritoneal, or pulmonary disease on this functional imaging study is clinically significant in the context of treatment planning. These findings support proceeding with neoadjuvant systemic therapy as the next management step, consistent with the clinical staging of borderline resectable, locoregional pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

NOTE: Mildly elevated serum glucose at time of FDG injection (134 mg/dL) in the setting of known diabetes mellitus may modestly limit sensitivity for small metastatic foci; however, overall image quality is deemed adequate for staging purposes.

1. KRAS — Missense Mutation
   Variant:             p.G12V (c.35G>T); exon 2, codon 12
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 42%
   Classification:      Pathogenic — Oncogenic Driver
   Functional significance: Substitution of glycine for valine at codon 12 of KRAS results in constitutive GTP-bound activation of the RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling cascades, rendering the RAS protein resistant to GAP-mediated GTP hydrolysis. KRAS G12V is the second most common activating KRAS point mutation in pancreatic cancer (after G12D) and is present in approximately 10-15% of KRAS-mutant PDAC cases.
   Prevalence in PDAC:  KRAS mutations occur in >90% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas; G12V represents approximately 25% of these.
   Clinical context:    KRAS G12V is a well-established oncogenic driver in PDAC. There are no approved direct KRAS G12V inhibitors as of this report for pancreatic adenocarcinoma, though investigational agents targeting KRAS G12V (e.g., MRTX1133, adagrasib analogs) are under active clinical investigation in PDAC. The VAF of 42% is consistent with a clonal event in the presence of approximately 40% tumor cellularity. This variant is NOT associated with any germline cancer predisposition syndrome.
   Therapy Implication: Investigational only; no FDA-approved targeted therapy available for KRAS G12V in pancreatic cancer.

2. TP53 — Missense Mutation
   Variant:             p.R248W (c.742C>T); exon 7, DNA-binding domain
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 38%
   Classification:      Pathogenic — Tumor Suppressor Gene, Loss-of-Function (dominant negative)
   Functional significance: Arginine 248 is a DNA-contact residue in the p53 DNA-binding domain; the R248W substitution is among the most common and functionally characterized TP53 hotspot mutations. This alteration confers loss of wild-type p53 transcriptional activity and, importantly, a dominant negative effect that can suppress the activity of any residual wild-type p53 from the second allele. R248W also confers gain-of-function (GOF) oncogenic properties including enhanced cell migration, drug resistance, and altered metabolic reprogramming. Loss of heterozygosity at the TP53 locus is common in PDAC and is consistent with the high VAF observed relative to tumor cellularity.
   Prevalence in PDAC:  TP53 mutations occur in 50-75% of PDAC; R248W is one of the most recurrent hotspot variants.
   Therapy Implication: No approved targeted therapies directly exploit TP53 R248W in pancreatic cancer. Investigational APR-246 (eprenetapopt) and related compounds restoring p53 function are not specifically active against GOF R248W in this context. TP53 mutation status may inform prognosis (generally adverse in PDAC).

3. SMAD4 — Homozygous Deletion
   Variant:             Copy number = 0 (homozygous deletion)
   Chromosomal locus:   18q21.2
   Classification:      Pathogenic — Tumor Suppressor Gene, Biallelic Inactivation
   Functional significance: SMAD4 (DPC4 — Deleted in Pancreatic Cancer locus 4) encodes the central mediator of canonical TGF-β/BMP signaling. Homozygous deletion of SMAD4 results in complete loss of SMAD4 protein, abrogating TGF-β-mediated tumor suppression. In pancreatic cancer, SMAD4 loss is associated with widespread metastatic dissemination and a more aggressive clinical phenotype. This molecular finding is concordant with the complete loss of SMAD4 protein expression by immunohistochemistry reported on the prior diagnostic biopsy (DPC4 IHC negative).
   Prevalence in PDAC:  SMAD4 is deleted or mutated in approximately 55% of PDAC; homozygous deletion accounts for approximately 30% of inactivating events.
   Therapy Implication: No approved targeted therapies. SMAD4 loss may have prognostic implications, and some evidence suggests SMAD4-null PDAC may be less likely to achieve durable locoregional control. SMAD4 status has been evaluated as a predictor of benefit from local therapy vs. systemic-only approaches (SMAD4 loss associated with higher systemic relapse risk).

4. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion
   Variant:             Copy number = 0 (homozygous deletion); affects CDKN2A (p16/INK4A) and CDKN2B (p15/INK4B) loci
   Chromosomal locus:   9p21.3
   Classification:      Pathogenic — Tumor Suppressor Gene, Biallelic Inactivation
   Functional significance: CDKN2A encodes the cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor p16/INK4A, a critical negative regulator of the CDK4/6-RB axis. Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A results in unrestrained CDK4/6 activity, leading to constitutive RB phosphorylation and release of E2F transcription factors driving cell cycle progression from G1 to S phase. Concomitant deletion of CDKN2B further impairs TGF-β-dependent growth arrest. This alteration is among the earliest and most prevalent events in pancreatic carcinogenesis, often arising in the precursor PanIN lesion stage.
   Prevalence in PDAC:  CDKN2A inactivation occurs in >90% of PDAC, predominantly via homozygous deletion or promoter hypermethylation.
   Therapy Implication: CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) have been investigated in PDAC with CDKN2A loss, but clinical benefit has been modest in unselected PDAC. CDKN2A loss may render the tumor theoretically more sensitive to CDK4/6-RB axis targeting in the context of clinical trials.

5. ATM — Frameshift Mutation  *** GERMLINE TESTING RECOMMENDED ***
   Variant:             p.V2716fs (c.8145del, single nucleotide deletion); exon 58, FAT/FATC domain
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 6%
   Classification:      Pathogenic (frameshift, predicted loss of function); Germline vs. Somatic: UNCERTAIN — germline testing of peripheral blood recommended
   Functional significance: ATM (Ataxia-Telangiectasia Mutated) encodes a serine/threonine kinase central to the DNA damage response, particularly double-strand break (DSB) repair via homologous recombination (HR) and non-homologous end joining (NHEJ). Frameshift mutations in ATM are predicted to produce a truncated, non-functional protein. Loss of ATM function impairs DSB repair, potentially conferring sensitivity to DNA-damaging agents (platinum compounds, PARP inhibitors). The VAF of 6% is low relative to the estimated tumor cellularity (~40%), which could reflect: (a) a germline heterozygous variant with loss of heterozygosity (LOH) not occurring in this tumor (expected VAF ~50% if clonal-germline without LOH, or lower if LOH in only a subpopulation); (b) a subclonal somatic mutation; or (c) technical factors. Given the low VAF and the pathogenicity of this frameshift, germline ATM testing is strongly recommended. Germline pathogenic ATM variants are associated with a 2- to 3-fold increased risk of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and with increased risks of breast, prostate, and other cancers. ATM-deficient tumors may have enhanced sensitivity to PARP inhibitors and platinum-based chemotherapy, which has therapeutic implications particularly in the advanced/metastatic setting.
   Clinical note:        If confirmed germline, family members (first-degree relatives) should be offered genetic counseling and cascade testing. Per NCCPA/ASCO guidelines, germline ATM pathogenic variants may qualify patients for enhanced pancreatic cancer surveillance programs in unaffected relatives.
   GERMLINE TESTING STRONGLY RECOMMENDED for this variant.

6. BRCA2 — Splice Site Variant  *** GERMLINE TESTING RECOMMENDED ***
   Variant:             c.8331+1G>A (canonical splice donor site, intron 18)
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 5%
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic — splice site variant affecting a canonical GT splice donor dinucleotide; Germline vs. Somatic: UNCERTAIN, likely subclonal somatic or technical artifact given low VAF — germline testing recommended
   Functional significance: The c.8331+1G>A variant disrupts the invariant +1 splice donor nucleotide of intron 18 of BRCA2, predicted to cause aberrant splicing with likely exon 18 retention or exon skipping, leading to a frameshift and premature stop codon. BRCA2 is a key mediator of homologous recombination repair; biallelic BRCA2 loss creates a state of BRCAness with increased sensitivity to platinum compounds and PARP inhibitors. In PDAC, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variants occur in approximately 4-7% of cases, particularly in Ashkenazi Jewish and other high-risk populations, and confer substantially elevated lifetime risk of pancreatic cancer. The very low VAF (5%) is below the level expected for a clonal somatic or germline event at the estimated tumor cellularity; this may represent a subclonal somatic event arising late in tumor evolution, an artifact of sequencing in the FFPE specimen, or a true low-level somatic event. Germline testing should be performed to exclude a germline origin.
   Note:                 If this variant is confirmed to be somatic only and subclonal, its clinical significance for therapeutic selection is limited. If confirmed germline, BRCA2 status would qualify the patient for olaparib maintenance therapy if metastatic disease were to develop in the future (per FDA labeling for BRCA-mutant pancreatic cancer), and for BRCA2-related cancer surveillance and family counseling.
   GERMLINE TESTING RECOMMENDED to clarify origin of this variant.

7. ARID1A — Nonsense Mutation
   Variant:             p.R1276* (c.3826C>T); exon 20
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 12%
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic — Tumor Suppressor, Loss of Function
   Functional significance: ARID1A encodes a component of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex (specifically BAF complex, AT-rich interactive domain 1A). Nonsense mutations such as p.R1276* are predicted to result in a truncated, non-functional protein, impairing chromatin remodeling and transcriptional regulation. ARID1A mutations occur in approximately 6-10% of PDAC and are associated with impaired DNA mismatch repair pathway interactions in some tumor types; however, this tumor is MSS, suggesting that ARID1A loss alone is insufficient to drive microsatellite instability in this context. There are currently no approved targeted therapies specifically directed against ARID1A-mutant pancreatic cancer. Preclinical data suggest that ARID1A-deficient tumors may have synthetic lethality with ARID1B inhibition or EZH2 inhibition, but these remain investigational. The subclonal VAF of 12% (below the expected clonal ~42% based on KRAS) suggests this mutation may have arisen as a secondary, clonal evolution event.

8. RNF43 — Frameshift Mutation
   Variant:             p.G659fs (c.1975delG); exon 9
   Variant Allele Fraction (VAF): 8%
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic — Potential Loss of Function
   Functional significance: RNF43 encodes a transmembrane E3 ubiquitin ligase that functions as a negative regulator of the Wnt/Frizzled signaling pathway by ubiquitinating Frizzled receptors for lysosomal degradation. Loss-of-function mutations in RNF43 lead to constitutive activation of the Wnt/β-catenin pathway. RNF43 frameshift mutations occur in approximately 5-10% of PDAC and are more commonly associated with pancreatic mucinous neoplasms/intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN)-associated adenocarcinoma, though they may occur in conventional PDAC. RNF43-mutant tumors may theoretically be sensitive to porcupine inhibitors (e.g., LGK-974, WNT-974) that block Wnt ligand secretion, but no such agents are currently approved. This low-VAF subclonal alteration may represent intratumoral heterogeneity or a late evolutionary event.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This tumor demonstrates the classic four-gene alteration signature of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma: concurrent KRAS activating mutation (G12V), TP53 missense mutation (R248W), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. This co-mutational signature is present in approximately 10-15% of PDAC cases and generally represents a fully evolved, transcriptionally dysregulated adenocarcinoma with poor prognosis in the absence of surgical resection. The presence of SMAD4 homozygous deletion in this borderline resectable context is noteworthy: SMAD4-null PDAC has been associated with hematogenous and peritoneal dissemination patterns in the neoadjuvant setting, and has been proposed as a biomarker to identify patients who may benefit more from prolonged systemic therapy prior to surgical reassessment. Concomitant CDKN2A homozygous deletion adds additional cell cycle dysregulation; together with SMAD4 loss, this profile represents a highly aggressive molecular phenotype.

There is no currently approved molecularly targeted therapy for this patient's tumor based on the detected somatic alterations. The tumor is MSS/TMB-Low/PD-L1 CPS 2, precluding immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy based on current FDA approvals. No NTRK, FGFR, or HER2 alterations are present.

The most actionable clinical implication of this report is the ATM frameshift variant (p.V2716fs, VAF 6%), which warrants urgent germline testing. Germline ATM pathogenic variants are associated with a modestly elevated risk of PDAC (approximately 2-3x population risk) and, more importantly in the therapeutic context, predict for enhanced sensitivity to DNA damage repair pathway-targeted agents including PARP inhibitors and platinum-based chemotherapy. While this is most immediately relevant in the metastatic disease setting, the identification of a germline ATM variant has implications for: (a) current treatment selection (platinum-containing chemotherapy regimens such as FOLFIRINOX may be particularly advantageous in ATM-deficient tumors); (b) potential eligibility for clinical trials evaluating PARP inhibitors in germline ATM-mutant PDAC; and (c) genetic counseling and cascade testing of first-degree family members at increased cancer risk.

The low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant (c.8331+1G>A, VAF 5%) is of uncertain significance in this tumor-only assay. If confirmed germline, this would identify the patient as a BRCA2 carrier with therapeutic implications (PARP inhibitor eligibility, clinical trial access). Germline testing is recommended. If confirmed somatic-only and subclonal, clinical impact on current treatment is limited.

Variant pathogenicity classification based on ClinVar, COSMIC, OncoKB (Memorial Sloan Kettering), CIViC database, and published literature. Copy number analysis performed using normalized read-depth ratio compared to a matched process-normal reference. Fusion detection by RNA-based hybrid capture and sequencing of selected intronic regions. TMB calculated from somatic non-synonymous coding mutations per megabase of sequenced coding genome, after filtering germline polymorphisms. MSI assessed by both fragment analysis (capillary electrophoresis) and NGS-based MSIsensor score. PD-L1 scored by certified pathologist using 22C3 pharmDx assay (Dako/Agilent) on the FFPE biopsy specimen.

This report reflects the genomic profile of the analyzed specimen and is limited by specimen quality, tumor heterogeneity, and the scope of the gene panel (324 genes; whole-genome alterations outside the panel are not detected). Results should be interpreted in the context of full clinical, pathological, and radiographic data by a qualified physician. This assay is for research and clinical decision support; it does not constitute a standalone treatment recommendation.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African female, retired schoolteacher, who presents for initial medical oncology consultation following recent diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). She was referred for multidisciplinary tumor board review and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old nonsmoker with a background of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who came to medical attention over the past several months with a constellation of symptoms that prompted abdominal imaging. She reports a 3-month history of progressive worsening of her previously well-controlled blood sugars, which her primary care physician initially attributed to dietary changes and medication non-adherence. In retrospect, this represents new-onset pancreatogenic diabetes — a recognized early harbinger of pancreatic malignancy — and was the pivotal symptom driving workup. She also endorses vague, dull epigastric and right upper quadrant discomfort that she described as "fullness" and attributed to her diabetes. She notes a 4–5 kg unintentional weight loss over this same period. She denied frank jaundice initially, though family members noted mild scleral icterus on one occasion. No pruritus. No acholic stools or dark urine at the time of initial presentation.

In response to worsening glycemic control, her PCP ordered abdominal ultrasonography, which demonstrated a hypoechoic mass in the head of the pancreas with associated pancreatic duct dilation. She was then urgently referred to gastroenterology, and a pancreatic-protocol multiphasic CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained. This revealed a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas with involvement of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over greater than 180 degrees of circumference and direct abutment of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with mild luminal narrowing. One peripancreatic lymph node measuring 1.2 cm was suspicious for metastatic involvement. There was moderate main pancreatic duct dilation to 6 mm and mild intrahepatic biliary dilation with a common bile duct diameter of 12 mm. No hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or distant organ metastases were identified on CT. A subsequent FDG-PET/CT confirmed an intensely FDG-avid pancreatic head mass (SUVmax 8.9) with one FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2) and no distant metastatic disease.

Tissue confirmation was obtained by endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy, which demonstrated moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (infiltrating duct carcinoma, grade 2) with prominent desmoplastic stroma. IHC showed CK7+, CK20−, CDX2−, MUC1+, and notably, loss of SMAD4 (DPC4) expression. Comprehensive genomic profiling via 324-gene next-generation sequencing panel revealed: KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, ARID1A p.R1276* (VAF 12%), RNF43 p.G659fs (VAF 8%), ATM p.V2716fs frameshift variant (VAF 6% — germline vs. somatic uncertain), and a low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant (c.8331+1G>A, VAF 5% — likely subclonal or technical artifact). TMB was low at 3 mutations/Mb, MSI status was MSS, and PD-L1 CPS was 2. No targetable fusions or HER2 amplification identified.

CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL (markedly elevated). CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. These values will serve as the baseline for tumor marker surveillance.

The case has been formally presented to the Multidisciplinary Pancreatic Cancer Tumor Board today with review of all imaging, pathology, and genomic data. The patient has been seen in clinic for this consultation and participates in treatment planning.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — Stage III (T4 N1 M0)

Ms. Osei-Bonsu presents with newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed, borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. The classification as borderline resectable is driven primarily by SMA involvement exceeding 180 degrees of circumference — a feature that by consensus criteria (AHPBA/SSAT/SSO, NCCN) places this tumor beyond technically resectable into borderline territory. Direct SMV contact with partial luminal narrowing is also noted. While immediate upfront surgery is not appropriate, the absence of distant metastatic disease and her good performance status make her a candidate for multimodality treatment with curative intent.

The molecular profile is typical for sporadic PDAC: KRAS G12V driver mutation (the most common KRAS codon 12 variant), TP53 R248W loss-of-function, and co-deletion of both SMAD4 and CDKN2A — a profile associated with aggressive biology but no immediately actionable therapeutic targets. Importantly, she is MSS/TMB-low, ruling out a checkpoint inhibitor strategy (Keytruda, nivo). SMAD4 loss portends a slightly higher risk of systemic dissemination and may temper expectations for local control alone, though we will proceed with curative intent. The ATM frameshift variant with low VAF is ambiguous; germline confirmation is pending and will be important for family counseling and, if confirmed, potential eligibility for PARP inhibitor maintenance strategies post-resection in future settings. The low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant is likely subclonal or artifactual, but germline testing is warranted to rule out actionable hereditary contribution.

PLAN FOR NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY:
After multidisciplinary discussion with surgical oncology, radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology, the consensus recommendation is for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy prior to any consideration of surgery or radiation. The rationale is multi-fold: (a) to achieve tumor reduction and ideally improve vascular anatomy, rendering the patient more favorably positioned for R0 resection; (b) to treat potential micrometastatic disease early; and (c) to serve as an in vivo test of disease biology — patients who progress on neoadjuvant therapy are unlikely to benefit from surgery.

Regimen: Modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX)
- Eloxatin 85 mg/m²
- Camptosar 150 mg/m² (dose-reduced from the standard 180 mg/m² given the patient's age of 66 and underlying type 2 diabetes, which may increase susceptibility to GI toxicity and neuropathy)
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m²
- 5FU 2400 mg/m² as a 46-hour continuous infusion
- Bolus 5-FU OMITTED (consistent with standard mFOLFIRINOX modification to reduce toxicity)
- Cycle every 14 days; planned 8 cycles (approximately 4 months)

Rationale for mFOLFIRINOX over gem/nab-pac: Given her ECOG 1 performance status, adequate organ function, and the potential benefit of the more active triplet backbone in the neoadjuvant setting, mFOLFIRINOX is preferred per current NCCN guidelines and institutional protocol. Published data (FOLFIRINOX vs. gem in metastatic PDAC; Conroy et al.) and neoadjuvant series support the superior activity of FOLFIRINOX-based regimens in patients who can tolerate them.

Supportive care and toxicity prophylaxis:
- Prophylactic pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) on day 2 of each cycle given the myelosuppressive potential of iri and Eloxatin
- Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, olanzapine 5 mg (NK1/5-HT3/steroid combination for highly emetogenic chemotherapy)
- Loperamide (Imodium) PRN for diarrhea with written management guidelines provided to patient
- Patient educated on cold sensitivity from oxali (avoidance of cold exposure to hands and mouth during and after infusions for first several days)
- Baseline peripheral neuropathy neurologic exam documented (no neuropathy at baseline)

2. BILIARY MONITORING AND INTERVENTION PLANNING
Total bilirubin currently 1.1 mg/dL, mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase. Although not requiring immediate biliary decompression, we will monitor LFTs closely. If bilirubin rises to 2 mg/dL or above, or if the patient develops symptomatic jaundice or cholangitis, biliary stenting (ERCP-guided metal stent preferred for durable drainage in a patient anticipating extended neoadjuvant treatment) will be arranged urgently through gastroenterology. She should be instructed to report any onset of jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stools, or fever.

3. GENETIC COUNSELING AND GERMLINE TESTING
The NGS panel identified an ATM p.V2716fs frameshift variant (VAF 6%) and a BRCA2 splice site variant c.8331+1G>A (VAF 5%). Both have low VAFs and are ambiguous regarding germline vs. somatic origin. Formal genetic counseling referral has been placed today. Germline testing will be sent (preferably blood or saliva-based germline panel). If ATM germline pathogenic variant is confirmed, this has implications for: (a) family member testing (autosomal dominant inheritance), (b) potential eligibility for PARP inhibitor strategies in maintained therapy contexts (ola in BRCA/ATM-mutated pancreatic cancer — POLO trial data support BRCA2, and emerging data for ATM in maintenance), and (c) ongoing surveillance considerations for breast and other cancers. Patient has been briefly counseled on this today; full discussion at genetic counseling appointment.

4. DIABETES MANAGEMENT OPTIMIZATION
HbA1c is 9.6%, indicating significantly worsening glycemic control — partially attributable to underlying pancreatogenic diabetes from pancreatic duct obstruction and tumor-related mechanisms. Metformin should be held during chemotherapy infusions (IV contrast exposure risk; also lactic acidosis risk in the setting of potential GI toxicity and dehydration). Endocrinology co-management referral placed for optimization of glycemic control during chemotherapy. Adequate diabetes management is important for chemotherapy tolerance, wound healing considerations for future surgery, and overall performance status.

5. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT
Patient has lost 4–5 kg over 3 months. Albumin is 3.6 g/dL. Evidence of early pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhea). Dietitian referral placed. Supplemental oral nutritional support (Ensure Plus or similar) encouraged. Consider empiric pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (e.g., Creon/pancrelipase) if steatorrhea is bothersome. Adequate nutritional status is critical for tolerance of mFOLFIRINOX and future surgical candidacy.

6. RESTAGING AND RESPONSE ASSESSMENT PLAN
CA 19-9 is established as baseline at 1,247 U/mL today. Tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA) will be checked with each cycle. Formal restaging with multiphasic pancreatic-protocol CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 2 months from start of treatment). Response assessment will guide continuation of therapy, modification of plan, or consideration of early progression.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PATIENT EDUCATION
Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her family (adult children accompanied her today) were counseled at length about the diagnosis, the intent of neoadjuvant therapy (to improve the chances of achieving a successful surgery with negative margins), the expected side effects of mFOLFIRINOX (nausea, fatigue, neuropathy, diarrhea, alopecia, myelosuppression), and the overall treatment trajectory. She understands that the ultimate goal is to achieve sufficient response to allow for a potentially curative surgery (Whipple procedure), while acknowledging that outcomes in pancreatic cancer are uncertain. She expressed a strong desire to pursue aggressive treatment and accepted the recommendation. She will have a dedicated nurse navigator and will have direct phone access to the oncology clinic for toxicity management.

Social work referral placed for psychosocial support. Patient resides alone but has strong family support and transportation arranged.

---

SUMMARY OF TUMOR BOARD RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Diagnosis confirmed: Borderline resectable PDAC, Stage III (T4 N1 M0)
- Neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) x 8 cycles recommended
- No upfront surgery at this time (SMA >180 degrees renders immediate resection unfeasible)
- Biliary decompression on standby if bilirubin rises
- Genetic counseling referral placed (ATM frameshift, BRCA2 low-VAF variant)
- Endocrinology referral for diabetes optimization
- Dietitian referral for nutritional support
- Restaging CT planned after 4 cycles
- ECOG 1; performance status adequate for mFOLFIRINOX
- No prior systemic therapy; treatment-naive at initiation
- CA 19-9 baseline: 1,247 U/mL
- Cancer present; intent curative

RESTAGING CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST (PANCREATIC PROTOCOL)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with known borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12V mutant, ATM germline pathogenic variant. Initially staged as T4 N1 M0 with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees and SMV contact with partial luminal narrowing. CA 19-9 at diagnosis 1,247 U/mL, declining to 620 U/mL after two cycles; now 310 U/mL after 4 cycles. Undergoing neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX. Presented for interval response assessment.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed on a 64-slice multidetector scanner following administration of 120 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast. Acquisitions obtained in the late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal) phase and portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal reformations generated. Thin-section axial series reconstructed at 1.25 mm intervals through the pancreatic bed with dedicated review of perivascular anatomy. No oral contrast administered per pancreatic protocol. Comparison made to prior multiphasic CT obtained at the time of initial diagnosis.

FINDINGS:

Pancreas:
There has been meaningful interval reduction in the size of the hypoenhancing mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process. The lesion now measures approximately 3.1 x 2.5 cm in the axial plane (previously 3.8 x 3.2 cm on the diagnostic study), representing a perceptible decrease across all measured dimensions. The mass remains hypoenhancing relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on both arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions, with a less prominent desmoplastic halo than appreciated on the prior examination. The margins of the tumor appear somewhat less infiltrative in character, though the lesion retains the irregular, spiculated morphology characteristic of ductal adenocarcinoma.

The main pancreatic duct proximal to the mass demonstrates residual mild dilation, measuring approximately 4 mm in greatest caliber (reduced from 6 mm on the prior study), consistent with partial relief of ductal obstruction. The pancreatic parenchyma upstream of the tumor shows mild volume loss with some fatty replacement changes, likely reflecting progressive atrophy in the setting of chronic ductal obstruction.

Vascular Relationships:
Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): On careful multiplanar review, tumor contact with the right lateral wall of the SMA has decreased substantially. The arc of soft tissue abutting the vessel circumference is now estimated at approximately 90 to 120 degrees — a notable improvement from the greater-than-180-degree circumferential contact documented at baseline. The fat plane between the tumor and the SMA, while not fully restored, is partially apparent on several consecutive axial and coronal images. No definitive arterial encasement or luminal irregularity of the SMA is identified. The proximal SMA remains patent with preserved luminal caliber.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Confluence: The SMV retains residual soft tissue contact at its right anterior surface at the level of the uncinate process; however, the previously noted mild luminal narrowing has improved. The venous lumen appears adequately preserved in caliber on portal venous phase images with no teardrop deformity or collateral venous formation to suggest hemodynamically significant obstruction. The portal vein confluence is patent. The splenic vein appears normal in caliber.

Celiac Axis and Hepatic Artery: No involvement of the celiac axis or proper hepatic artery. Normal origins and caliber. No arterial encasement.

Lymph Nodes:
The previously noted peripancreatic lymph node along the superior mesenteric vessel pedicle has decreased in short-axis diameter to approximately 0.7 cm (previously 1.2 cm), no longer meeting size criteria for suspicion of nodal involvement. No new enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, celiac, retroperitoneal, or pelvic regions.

Bile Ducts and Gallbladder:
The common bile duct measures approximately 9 mm in caliber (previously 12 mm), with modest improvement in biliary decompression. Mild residual intrahepatic biliary prominence is noted, predominantly involving the left lobe; this is stable to slightly improved. The gallbladder appears unremarkable without wall thickening, stones, or pericholecystic fluid. No biliary stent is in place.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on either the arterial or portal venous phase. No hepatic cysts, hemangiomas, or hypervascular or hypovascular lesions to suggest metastatic involvement. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in appearance without nodularity or enlargement.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys enhance normally and symmetrically. No hydronephrosis, cortical lesions, or perinephric abnormality. Mild cortical thinning bilaterally, likely representing age-related change.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity, omental thickening, or mesenteric soft tissue implants to suggest carcinomatosis. The mesenteric fat planes appear clean without stellate retraction or seeding.

Bowel:
Small and large bowel loops are within normal limits for caliber and wall thickness. No obstruction or pneumatosis. The duodenum appears patent.

Bony Structures:
No destructive osseous lesions identified within the visualized skeletal structures. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine consistent with age.

IMPRESSION:

1. Interval reduction in the size of the pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, now measuring approximately 3.1 x 2.5 cm compared with 3.8 x 3.2 cm at initial staging — a meaningful decrease in tumor bulk following four cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX, consistent with a partial radiographic response.

2. Improved vascular geometry at the superior mesenteric artery: the arc of tumor-vessel contact has decreased to an estimated 90-120 degrees of circumference, compared with greater-than-180-degree contact at baseline. While a clear fat plane has not been fully restored, the anatomic relationship is substantially more favorable than at presentation.

3. Improved SMV patency: residual soft tissue contact at the SMV is present but the previously appreciated luminal narrowing has resolved. No collateral formation.

4. The previously suspicious peripancreatic lymph node has decreased to 0.7 cm and is no longer convincingly enlarged.

5. Modest improvement in common bile duct caliber and mild reduction in upstream pancreatic ductal dilation, reflecting partial decompression of the biliary and ductal systems.

6. No hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or ascites. No new sites of disease.

Overall, these findings reflect a favorable oncologic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The evolving vascular anatomy warrants continued multidisciplinary assessment regarding timing and feasibility of surgical resection.
